1	# ADR-004: MVP Native Tool Scope
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The Tool Registry MVP must prove more than metadata listing. It must demonstrate that AGH-owned tools can be discovered, authorized, invoked through the hosted MCP surface, called through CLI/HTTP/UDS contracts, routed through one dispatch pipeline, and observed.
10	
11	The smallest read-only bootstrap toolset would prove catalog mechanics, but it would not prove meaningful autonomy. AGH's product direction requires agents to manage coordination and task flows, so network and task tools should be represented in the first implementation slice.
12	
13	At the same time, skill installation is a separate supply-chain surface. It requires stricter install policy, trust tiers, approval flows, and scanner decisions. It should not be bundled into the first registry execution proof unless the supply-chain work is explicitly scoped.
14	
15	## Decision
16	
17	The MVP native tool scope includes the bootstrap catalog/skill tools plus network and task tools:
18	
19	- `agh__tool_list`
20	- `agh__tool_search`
21	- `agh__tool_info`
22	- `agh__skill_list`
23	- `agh__skill_search`
24	- `agh__skill_view`
25	- `agh__network_peers`
26	- `agh__network_send`
27	- `agh__task_*`
28	
29	`agh__task_*` will be specified in the TechSpec as a bounded set of first-class task operations, not as an unreviewed wildcard implementation. Candidate operations include list/get/create/update/claim/release/complete where they map to existing or planned AGH task primitives.
30	
31	Skill install/remove/update tools are not included in the MVP native tool scope unless a later decision explicitly adds the required supply-chain and approval work.
32	
33	## Consequences
34	
35	The MVP must include both read-only and mutating tools. The registry must model risk, read-only/destructive/open-world flags, permission checks, and policy gates from the first implementation.
36	
37	Network and task tools must use the same registry dispatch path as catalog tools. They must not call around policy, availability, hooks, result budgeting, or telemetry.
38	
39	The hosted MCP server must expose only the tool subset authorized for the session. Agent-visible discovery must not advertise network/task tools to sessions that lack the required permission atoms.
40	
41	QA must include real scenario coverage for:
42	
43	- listing and searching tools,
44	- viewing a skill body through `agh__skill_view`,
45	- listing peers,
46	- sending a network message through `agh__network_send` with permission enforcement,
47	- creating/updating or otherwise exercising the bounded `agh__task_*` set,
48	- proving unauthorized sessions cannot see or call mutating tools.
49	
50	## Rejected Alternatives
51	
52	### Read-only bootstrap only
53	
54	This would be safer and simpler, but it would leave the registry unproven for AGH's coordination and autonomy use cases.
55	
56	### Bootstrap plus skill install
57	
58	This would improve agent self-service, but it introduces supply-chain risk that belongs in a dedicated policy/scanning/install decision.
59	
60	### Foundation only
61	
62	This would create the architecture without proving the agent-first experience that motivated the Tool Registry work.
63	
64	## Evidence
65	
66	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/synthesis.md`: recommends a small bootstrap set and identifies network/task tools as later groups.
67	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/analysis/analysis_skills_tools_registry.md`: prior gaps identify network and task tools as strategically important agent-callable surfaces.
68	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/analysis/analysis_tools_security.md`: mutating and open-world tools require stronger permission and security gates before broad exposure.
69	
```

1	# ADR-001: Extension Tool Execution Boundary
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	AGH's Tool Registry must support native/bundled tools and tools contributed by extensions. Competitor research shows three broad patterns:
10	
11	- Hermes allows in-process plugin/tool registration, which is flexible but broadens daemon compromise risk.
12	- Claude Code routes plugin-contributed tools primarily through MCP, keeping third-party tools behind a protocol boundary.
13	- OpenClaw uses manifest-first static discovery and runtime materialization through plugin/MCP adapters.
14	- AGH already lets extension manifests publish static `resources.tools`, but those records are currently metadata-only.
15	
16	The registry foundation must preserve AGH's extensibility goal without letting arbitrary extension code run inside the daemon process as a first implementation step.
17	
18	## Decision
19	
20	Extension-contributed tools will use a manifest-first, out-of-process execution boundary for the MVP.
21	
22	Extensions may declare tool descriptors in their manifest so AGH can discover, index, authorize, and show them without executing extension code. A declared extension tool becomes executable only when it is backed by an approved out-of-process adapter:
23	
24	- MCP server adapter,
25	- extension sidecar / Host API adapter,
26	- subprocess adapter managed by AGH runtime supervision,
27	- future bridge SDK adapter with equivalent process/protocol isolation.
28	
29	AGH built-in tools may register in-process because they are compiled into the daemon binary and reviewed as daemon code.
30	
31	Third-party extension handlers will not run in-process in the daemon for the MVP.
32	
33	## Consequences
34	
35	The registry must separate descriptor records from executable handles. A tool can be installed and discoverable while still being unavailable or non-executable until its backend is healthy and authorized.
36	
37	Extension manifests need backend metadata or a follow-up mechanism that binds static tool declarations to sidecar/MCP/subprocess handles.
38	
39	Dispatch must recheck availability, authorization, and backend health at call time. Discovery filtering is not a security boundary.
40	
41	The MVP can support extension tool discovery before full extension tool invocation. This keeps the foundation extensible while avoiding a daemon plugin ABI or in-process third-party execution model.
42	
43	## Rejected Alternatives
44	
45	### Metadata-only extension tools
46	
47	Keeping extension tools discoverable but never executable in the MVP would reduce scope, but it would not prove the registry's adapter model. It remains acceptable as a staged implementation path for individual extension backends, but not as the architectural boundary.
48	
49	### Trusted in-process extension handlers
50	
51	Allowing trusted or bundled extensions to register in-process handlers would be faster for first-party bundles, but it creates a second execution model and risks pressure to admit third-party handlers later. Built-ins should remain daemon code; extensions should cross a process/protocol boundary.
52	
53	## Evidence
54	
55	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_openclaw.md`: manifest-first discovery and runtime materialization.
56	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_claude-code.md`: plugin tools primarily flow through MCP adapters.
57	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_hermes.md`: in-process plugin/tool registration is flexible but mismatched with AGH's desired safety model.
58	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_agh_current_state.md`: AGH extension manifests already publish static tool metadata but have no executable registry handle.
59	
```

1	# ADR-002: Session Tool Exposure Path
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The Tool Registry must be agent-manageable, not only an internal daemon API. AGH manages external ACP-compatible runtimes, so it cannot assume direct control over every provider's internal model API tool loop.
10	
11	The registry still needs a model-visible path for AGH-owned tools such as `agh__tool_search` and `agh__skill_view`. Competitor research suggests MCP is the most portable first boundary:
12	
13	- Claude Code and other runtimes already understand MCP tools.
14	- MCP keeps tool exposure protocol-based rather than driver-specific.
15	- MCP can be backed by the same registry dispatch path as CLI, HTTP, and UDS.
16	- Runtimes without MCP can still reach AGH through CLI/UDS fallback surfaces.
17	
18	## Decision
19	
20	The MVP will expose AGH-native registry tools through an AGH-hosted local MCP server plus shared CLI, HTTP, and UDS contracts.
21	
22	The daemon will own one registry contract and dispatch pipeline. Surfaces call into that same pipeline:
23	
24	- hosted MCP server for model-visible AGH tools in runtimes that support MCP,
25	- CLI commands for operator and agent fallback use,
26	- HTTP API for web/operator clients,
27	- UDS API for local trusted clients and internal AGH tools.
28	
29	Direct driver/ACP injection can be added later as an optimization for runtimes that support it, but it is not the MVP exposure path.
30	
31	## Consequences
32	
33	AGH-native tools must be representable as MCP tools without losing policy, availability, hook, telemetry, and result-budget behavior.
34	
35	The registry contract types must be shared below all surfaces rather than copied into each transport.
36	
37	Session start should be able to attach the hosted AGH MCP server for agents whose runtime supports MCP. Agents/runtimes without MCP remain supported through CLI/UDS fallback.
38	
39	The TechSpec must define how the hosted MCP server is authorized, scoped to the session/workspace, and prevented from bypassing registry dispatch.
40	
41	Live catalog deltas can be designed as a later driver capability. The MVP can refresh the hosted MCP server's tool list and expose search/list tools through the registry.
42	
43	## Rejected Alternatives
44	
45	### CLI/UDS only
46	
47	This would be simpler and still agent-operable through terminal tools, but it would not provide native tool calls for runtimes with MCP support and would leave the main "last mile" gap partially open.
48	
49	### Direct ACP/driver injection first
50	
51	This could be cleaner for a single provider, but it is less portable and would force the first implementation into provider-specific behavior.
52	
53	### HTTP/UDS only
54	
55	This would build the management API but delay the session-visible tool surface, making the foundation less useful to autonomous agents.
56	
57	## Evidence
58	
59	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_claude-code.md`: MCP tools are adapted into the same local tool contract and refreshed dynamically.
60	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_openclaw.md`: MCP is a provider backend for plugin/bundle tools.
61	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_agh_current_state.md`: AGH already resolves MCP sidecars and has CLI/HTTP/UDS-style management surfaces elsewhere.
62	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_claude_code_ideas.md`: AGH should avoid assuming direct LLM API control while still exposing AGH-owned tools through provider-neutral surfaces.
63	
```

1	# ADR-003: Runtime Registry Package Boundary
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	AGH already has `internal/tools`, but it currently defines only metadata records and a list-only provider interface. The Tool Registry foundation needs runtime concerns: executable handles, provider adapters, availability, policy projection, dispatch, hooks, telemetry, result budgeting, and hosted MCP exposure.
10	
11	The registry also needs to compose with skills for agent-facing discovery operations such as `agh__tool_search`, `agh__skill_list`, and `agh__skill_view`. That creates a package-boundary question: should the runtime registry live in `internal/tools`, in a new broad `internal/catalog`, or in daemon orchestration code?
12	
13	## Decision
14	
15	`internal/tools` will own the runtime Tool Registry contracts and execution path.
16	
17	The package will evolve from metadata-only records into the home for:
18	
19	- tool descriptors and source/provenance types,
20	- executable handles and providers,
21	- availability and reason codes,
22	- policy projection for tool views,
23	- central `Registry.Call` dispatch,
24	- result normalization and result-budget metadata,
25	- provider adapters for built-ins, MCP, extension sidecars, subprocesses, and future bridges.
26	
27	A thin `internal/catalog` facade may compose `internal/tools` and `internal/skills` for cross-domain list/search/view surfaces and for AGH-native tools such as `agh__tool_search` and `agh__skill_view`.
28	
29	The daemon composition root will wire providers and dependencies, but it will not own registry logic.
30	
31	## Consequences
32	
33	Tool execution remains in the tool domain instead of a broad catalog domain. This keeps dispatch, policy, availability, and telemetry testable without booting the daemon.
34	
35	`internal/catalog` stays small and read-oriented. It coordinates cross-domain discovery and progressive disclosure, but does not become a second execution engine.
36	
37	Existing `internal/tools.Tool` resource compatibility must be handled through clear type splits rather than by adding runtime function fields to resource records. The TechSpec should distinguish cold resource specs from runtime descriptors and handles.
38	
39	Daemon boot must register providers explicitly, following AGH's composition-root discipline.
40	
41	## Rejected Alternatives
42	
43	### New `internal/catalog` owns everything
44	
45	This would centralize skills and tools, but it would also mix skill content loading, tool dispatch, extension adapter logic, search, and policy into one large package too early.
46	
47	### Daemon-owned registry logic
48	
49	Keeping registry logic inside daemon orchestration would reduce package churn, but it would make dispatch harder to test and would couple tool semantics to boot wiring.
50	
51	## Evidence
52	
53	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_agh_current_state.md`: `internal/tools` is currently metadata-only while daemon boot already owns resource projection.
54	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_goclaw.md`: GoClaw keeps executable tool contracts and policy close to the tools package.
55	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/synthesis.md`: recommends `internal/tools` for runtime contracts and a thin catalog facade for cross-skill/tool search.
56	- `internal/CLAUDE.md`: AGH favors interfaces where consumed and composition-root wiring over daemon package logic accumulation.
57	
```

1	# ADR-005: ACP Approval Policy Integration
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	AGH already has a system-level ACP tool approval policy exposed in settings and enforced by the ACP tool host:
10	
11	- `deny-all`
12	- `approve-reads`
13	- `approve-all`
14	
15	The Tool Registry will add per-tool metadata and policy, including read-only, destructive, open-world, source, toolset, session permission atoms, and extension/MCP grants. If this registry policy is designed as a parallel approval system, AGH can produce contradictory states such as:
16	
17	- system policy says `deny-all`, but a tool-level policy says allow;
18	- system policy says `approve-reads`, but a mutating tool claims read-only;
19	- system policy says `approve-all`, but a session/agent intentionally narrows permissions;
20	- hosted MCP exposes a tool the ACP host would later block.
21	
22	The registry policy must integrate with the existing ACP policy rather than bypass it.
23	
24	## Decision
25	
26	The ACP `permissions.mode` policy is the system approval ceiling for session-visible tool execution.
27	
28	Tool Registry policy operates below that ceiling as a more granular filter. It can narrow, classify, require approval, or deny a tool, but it cannot silently grant more authority than the effective system/session ACP policy allows.
29	
30	The effective decision order is:
31	
32	1. Resolve the system/session ACP approval mode.
33	2. Resolve agent/session lineage tool permission atoms.
34	3. Resolve registry visibility and allow/deny/toolset policy.
35	4. Resolve source/risk defaults for built-in, extension, MCP, and dynamic tools.
36	5. Resolve tool descriptor risk flags: read-only, destructive, open-world, requires interaction.
37	6. Run availability checks.
38	7. Run pre-call hooks.
39	8. Dispatch only if the combined decision is allowed or explicitly approved.
40	
41	`approve-all` is permissive but not a bypass of explicit denies. It removes automatic ACP prompting for allowed tools, but registry deny rules, unavailable state, session lineage restrictions, hooks, and source grants still apply.
42	
43	`approve-reads` auto-approves only tools classified as read-only by the registry and allowed by session policy. Mutating, destructive, open-world, network-send, and task-write tools require explicit approval or explicit session/tool policy that the approval layer recognizes.
44	
45	`deny-all` denies by default. The registry may still list operator-visible tools with reasons, but session-visible execution requires explicit approval/allowance through the existing ACP approval path or an equivalent local approval surface.
46	
47	Agent frontmatter and session lineage can lower permissions relative to system policy. They cannot raise permissions above the system approval ceiling.
48	
49	## Consequences
50	
51	The registry must compute an `EffectiveToolDecision` instead of storing a single boolean. The decision should include:
52	
53	- system approval mode,
54	- session/agent policy result,
55	- registry policy result,
56	- source/risk default result,
57	- availability result,
58	- hook result,
59	- final visibility decision,
60	- final execution decision,
61	- user/operator-facing reason codes.
62	
63	The hosted AGH MCP server must expose only tools allowed by the effective visibility decision for that session. Dispatch must still revalidate the effective execution decision.
64	
65	Tool descriptors must classify read-only vs mutating accurately. A mutating tool mislabeled as read-only is a correctness and security bug.
66	
67	The web settings UI text remains true: `approve-all` auto-approves tool calls, but agents and registry policy can lower permissions. The TechSpec should clarify that "auto-approved" does not mean "all registered tools are visible and executable regardless of registry policy."
68	
69	## Rejected Alternatives
70	
71	### Registry policy bypasses ACP policy
72	
73	This would create inconsistent behavior between ACP-native tools, AGH-hosted MCP tools, and CLI/UDS calls. It would also make the existing settings UI misleading.
74	
75	### Registry policy replaces ACP policy
76	
77	This would require redesigning existing ACP permission handling and settings before the Tool Registry can ship. The MVP should integrate with the existing model and extend it.
78	
79	### Tool-level policy alone controls execution
80	
81	Per-tool policy is necessary but insufficient. Session lineage, system approval mode, hooks, availability, and source grants all affect whether a call is safe and authorized.
82	
83	## Evidence
84	
85	- `internal/acp/permission.go:75-132`: ACP policy defaults, path validation, and decisions for `approve-all`, `approve-reads`, and `deny-all`.
86	- `internal/acp/tool_host.go:64-83`: local tool host is constructed with an ACP permission mode.
87	- `web/src/routes/_app/settings/general.tsx:307-315`: settings UI describes the three tool approval policies.
88	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_claude-code.md`: permission should be an ordered pipeline rather than a tool-local boolean.
89	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_goclaw.md`: runtime grants should be rechecked at execution time.
90	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/synthesis.md`: dispatch must recheck availability and authorization and use one central pipeline.
91	
```

1	# ADR-006: Tool Visibility by Surface
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The Tool Registry will track more states than "exists" or "does not exist." A tool may be registered but disabled, unauthorized, unavailable, unhealthy, missing configuration, missing an MCP backend, blocked by session policy, denied by ACP approval mode, or conflicted by name.
10	
11	Different consumers need different views:
12	
13	- operators need diagnostics and reason codes to fix configuration and extension problems;
14	- agents need a low-noise callable surface that does not invite impossible calls;
15	- dispatch still needs to revalidate because discovery visibility is not a security boundary.
16	
17	## Decision
18	
19	Operator surfaces show unavailable and unauthorized tools with reason codes. Session-visible and model-visible surfaces expose only tools that are visible and callable for the effective session context.
20	
21	Operator surfaces include:
22	
23	- CLI,
24	- HTTP API,
25	- Web UI,
26	- privileged UDS/operator views.
27	
28	Session-visible/model-visible surfaces include:
29	
30	- the AGH-hosted MCP tool list exposed to an agent session,
31	- any future direct ACP/driver tool injection,
32	- non-privileged session-scoped UDS catalog views.
33	
34	The registry must compute both:
35	
36	- `OperatorToolView`: includes all registered tools plus state, reason codes, source/provenance, policy diagnostics, conflict diagnostics, and availability details.
37	- `SessionToolView`: includes only tools that pass effective visibility and execution preconditions for that session.
38	
39	Dispatch must revalidate the full effective execution decision even when a tool was present in `SessionToolView`.
40	
41	## Consequences
42	
43	Agents are not shown tools that they cannot call in the current session. This avoids prompt/tool-call noise and reduces attempts to invoke unavailable tools.
44	
45	Operators can still debug why a tool is not appearing to an agent, including whether the cause is ACP approval mode, session lineage, agent policy, source grants, extension health, MCP health, missing config, or a conflict.
46	
47	CLI/HTTP endpoints need an explicit scope or view mode. For example:
48	
49	- operator default: include unavailable tools and reasons;
50	- session-scoped query: return the same filtered view that the hosted MCP server would expose.
51	
52	The hosted MCP server must use `SessionToolView`, not raw registry contents.
53	
54	## Rejected Alternatives
55	
56	### Everyone sees unavailable tools
57	
58	This improves agent planning transparency but increases noise and risks inducing models to call tools that the daemon will reject.
59	
60	### Hide unavailable tools everywhere
61	
62	This is clean for fail-closed execution, but it makes operator troubleshooting poor and hides extension/MCP/config problems.
63	
64	### Configurable per surface in MVP
65	
66	This offers maximum flexibility but creates a larger behavior matrix before the registry foundation is stable.
67	
68	## Evidence
69	
70	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_hermes.md`: availability filtering is useful for model-visible definitions.
71	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_claude-code.md`: request-time tool pools are context-specific and filtered before model exposure.
72	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/analysis_openclaw.md`: lifecycle and policy states need diagnostics while agent projections should be policy-filtered.
73	- `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/synthesis.md`: discovery can hide unavailable/unauthorized tools from agents while operator surfaces show reasons, but dispatch still rechecks.
74	
```

1	# ADR-007: Canonical Tool ID Format
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted.
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	AGH needs one stable tool identifier that can be used across the runtime registry, policy rules, CLI, HTTP, UDS, telemetry, hooks, audit logs, and the AGH-hosted MCP surface.
10	
11	Earlier options considered dotted internal IDs such as `agh.skill.view` plus a separate MCP-safe wire alias such as `agh_skill_view`. ACP/MCP compatibility research showed this would work technically, but it creates two strong names for the same tool and increases the chance of policy, audit, telemetry, or dispatch code using the wrong identity.
12	
13	The identifier also needs to remain compatible with provider and host constraints. MCP allows dots in tool names, but common model tool/function APIs are stricter and accept letters, digits, underscores, and hyphens with a 64-character limit. AGH should choose a lowest-common-denominator format for callable tool IDs rather than rely on a more permissive protocol layer.
14	
15	Claude Code uses the `mcp__server__tool` convention for MCP tools. This gives an explicit namespace boundary while staying inside provider-safe characters.
16	
17	## Decision
18	
19	AGH will use one canonical public `ToolID` format across every surface:
20	
21	```text
22	<segment>( "__" <segment> )*
23	```
24	
25	Each segment must match:
26	
27	```text
28	[a-z][a-z0-9_]*
29	```
30	
31	Global constraints:
32	
33	- maximum length: 64 characters;
34	- lowercase ASCII only;
35	- digits allowed after the first character of each segment;
36	- `_` allowed inside a segment;
37	- `__` is reserved exclusively as a segment separator;
38	- no dot;
39	- no hyphen;
40	- no uppercase;
41	- no empty segment;
42	- no leading or trailing `_` inside a segment if it would create an empty separator ambiguity.
43	
44	Examples:
45	
46	```text
47	agh__tool_list
48	agh__tool_search
49	agh__tool_info
50	agh__skill_list
51	agh__skill_view
52	agh__network_peers
53	agh__network_send
54	agh__task_list
55	agh__task_read
56	ext__linear__search
57	ext__linear__create_issue
58	mcp__github__create_issue
59	mcp__context7__query_docs
60	```
61	
62	`ToolID` is the identity used by:
63	
64	- registry descriptors;
65	- provider registration;
66	- policy allow/deny rules;
67	- toolsets;
68	- CLI commands;
69	- HTTP and UDS APIs;
70	- hooks;
71	- telemetry and audit logs;
72	- hosted MCP `Tool.name`;
73	- dispatch requests.
74	
75	AGH will not use a second wire alias for the same tool in the MVP. Display titles are UI-only and do not participate in policy, authorization, conflict resolution, or dispatch.
76	
77	Source/provenance remains structured metadata, not an alternate identity:
78	
79	```json
80	{
81	  "id": "mcp__github__create_issue",
82	  "source": {
83	    "kind": "mcp",
84	    "serverName": "github",
85	    "rawToolName": "create_issue"
86	  }
87	}
88	```
89	
90	AGH may show a shorter display title such as `Create Issue`, but the canonical ID remains `mcp__github__create_issue`.
91	
92	## Collision Rules
93	
94	Registration and session projection must fail closed:
95	
96	- If two providers produce the same `ToolID`, the later registration is rejected or marked `conflicted`.
97	- If sanitizing an external MCP/server/extension tool name would collide with an existing `ToolID`, the candidate tool is marked `conflicted` and is not exposed to model-visible surfaces.
98	- AGH must not silently truncate, overwrite, or choose "last writer wins".
99	- Operator surfaces may show conflicted tools with reason codes and provenance.
100	- Session/model-visible surfaces expose only non-conflicted callable tools.
101	
102	## Consequences
103	
104	Positive:
105	
106	- One identifier works across registry, wire, policy, telemetry, and dispatch.
107	- No dotted-to-wire alias mapping is needed in the MVP.
108	- Namespace boundaries remain visible through reserved `__`.
109	- The format is compatible with stricter provider tool-name constraints.
110	- Policy patterns stay simple, for example `agh__skill_*` and `mcp__github__*`.
111	
112	Tradeoffs:
113	
114	- Dotted names such as `agh.skill.view` are more visually familiar for namespace trees, but they are not provider-safe enough to use as callable IDs.
115	- `__` is less aesthetically clean than dots, but it avoids dual identity.
116	- Raw external names must be preserved in `SourceRef` for exact provenance and debugging.
117	
118	## Follow-Ups
119	
120	- The TechSpec must update all tool examples to this format.
121	- The registry validator must enforce the grammar.
122	- Extension and MCP adapters must sanitize external names deterministically and report conflicts.
123	- Policy matching must treat `__` as an identity segment separator and `_` as a normal segment character.
124	
```

1	# Tool Registry Foundation TechSpec
2	
3	## Executive Summary
4	
5	This TechSpec designs AGH's Tool Registry as a daemon-owned runtime service, not as a static list of built-in commands. The registry will unify tool identity, discovery, availability, policy, execution, hooks, telemetry, extension descriptors, MCP adapters, and session-visible exposure through one central dispatch pipeline.
6	
7	There is no existing `_prd.md` for this task. The scope is based on the user request, competitor research under `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/analysis/`, accepted ADRs under `.compozy/tasks/tools-registry/adrs/`, current AGH code exploration, and the prior autonomous skills/tools registry gap analysis.
8	
9	The primary architectural trade-off is to make AGH-native tools visible to sessions through an AGH-hosted local MCP server in the MVP instead of trying to invent an ACP-specific registry. ACP does not define a callable tool registry; it defines session lifecycle, MCP bootstrap, permission callbacks, and tool-call observations. MCP provides the programmatic `Tool.name`, so AGH will expose its canonical `ToolID` directly as the hosted MCP tool name.
10	
11	The foundation will support native/bundled tools and extension-installed tools. Built-in AGH tools may execute in-process. Third-party extension tools must stay out-of-process through MCP, extension sidecar/Host API, subprocess adapter, or a future bridge adapter. Extension manifests can install tool descriptors immediately; execution becomes available only when a healthy authorized backend handle exists.
12	
13	## MVP Boundary Statement
14	
15	MVP boundary: implementation steps 1-13 build the Tool Registry foundation, AGH-hosted MCP session exposure, native bootstrap tools, extension descriptor projection, shared CLI/HTTP/UDS surfaces, policy/availability enforcement, hooks, observability, docs, and verification. This MVP proves the registry as an executable daemon primitive without replacing every ACP provider-native tool.
16	
17	Post-MVP work deferred to later TechSpecs:
18	
19	- direct driver-specific tool injection outside hosted MCP;
20	- full shell/browser/file tool replacement for ACP runtimes;
21	- remote peer tool execution over AGH Network;
22	- provider-specific deferred schema loading such as Anthropic `tool_reference`;
23	- broad marketplace signing/trust overhaul;
24	- skill install/remove/update tools;
25	- bridge SDK executable tool adapters;
26	- client-supplied ACP `mcpServers` as session-scoped registry sources.
27	
28	Explicitly out of scope for this TechSpec:
29	
30	- in-process third-party extension handlers;
31	- silent compatibility aliases for dotted tool IDs;
32	- policy bypasses for `approve-all`;
33	- partial surface delivery where CLI/HTTP ships without UDS, docs, codegen, and hosted MCP parity;
34	- storing matchable ownership or authorization state inside opaque JSON metadata blobs.
35	
36	## Architectural Boundaries
37	
38	`internal/daemon` remains the only composition root. It may import and compose `internal/tools`, `internal/skills`, `internal/mcp`, `internal/extension`, `internal/hooks`, `internal/session`, `internal/network`, `internal/task`, `internal/api/*`, and config/resource stores. No package may import `internal/daemon`.
39	
40	Package import boundaries:
41	
42	- `internal/tools` owns `ToolID`, descriptors, providers, registry, policy interfaces, availability, dispatch contracts, and result normalization. It must not import `internal/daemon`, `internal/api/*`, `internal/cli`, `internal/extension`, `internal/session`, `internal/network`, or `internal/task`.
43	- `internal/catalog`, if added, is a thin composition-facing facade over `internal/tools` and `internal/skills`. It must not own tool dispatch or policy.
44	- `internal/extension` may publish cold tool descriptors and backend metadata through resources. It must not execute third-party tool handlers in-process and must not import registry internals beyond public `internal/tools` descriptor/resource contracts.
45	- `internal/mcp` may adapt MCP tools and host the AGH MCP stdio proxy. All AGH-owned calls must enter `internal/tools.Registry.Call` through UDS or an injected interface; MCP code must not duplicate dispatch policy.
46	- `internal/api/core` owns transport-independent handlers. `internal/api/httpapi` and `internal/api/udsapi` only register routes and transport concerns.
47	- `internal/cli` calls UDS/HTTP client methods and does not import runtime registry implementations.
48	- `internal/hooks` owns typed hook payloads and execution. Hooks dispatch at the registry call site; no code may tail event tables to trigger tool hooks.
49	- `internal/store` may persist session lineage permission atoms and events. It must not decide tool policy; it validates and stores normalized atoms.
50	- `internal/session` can receive hosted MCP server config and session projections through interfaces. It must not implement a parallel tool registry.
51	
52	Boundaries to update in implementation:
53	
54	- If `internal/catalog` or a new `internal/mcp` subpackage is added, update `magefile.go` package boundary checks in the same change.
55	- Any OpenAPI/contract change must co-ship generated `openapi/agh.json` and `web/src/generated/agh-openapi.d.ts`.
56	- Any CLI surface must co-ship HTTP/UDS parity unless the spec explicitly marks the surface CLI-only. This TechSpec has no CLI-only tool surfaces.
57	
58	## System Architecture
59	
60	### Component Overview
61	
62	| Component | Responsibility | Boundary |
63	|---|---|---|
64	| `internal/tools` runtime registry | Owns canonical `ToolID`, descriptors, providers, availability projection, policy evaluation, dispatch, result normalization, and telemetry hooks | Does not import `daemon`, `api`, or `cli`; composed by `internal/daemon` |
65	| Cold `tool` resources | Persist desired-state tool metadata from extensions, bundles, and future dynamic producers | Metadata only; no function pointers or execution handles |
66	| Built-in tool provider | Registers AGH-native tools such as tool search, skill view, network peers/send, and bounded task tools | In-process only because it ships inside the daemon binary |
67	| Extension descriptor provider | Converts extension-published tool resources into runtime descriptors | Does not execute extension code during discovery |
68	| MCP adapter provider | Discovers/calls MCP-backed tools and preserves raw server/tool names in `SourceRef` | Fails closed on health, auth, or name collision problems |
69	| `internal/catalog` facade | Thin cross-domain list/search/view facade over tools and skills | Optional coordination layer; runtime tool dispatch remains in `internal/tools` |
70	| Policy engine | Combines ACP approval mode, session lineage, agent policy, source/risk defaults, registry allow/deny, toolsets, availability, and hooks | Produces structured effective decisions, never a single ambiguous boolean |
71	| AGH-hosted MCP proxy | Exposes session-callable AGH tools as MCP tools using canonical `ToolID` names | Runs through `agh tool mcp --session <id>` and proxies to daemon over UDS |
72	| API/CLI surfaces | Expose machine-readable list/search/info/invoke/status behavior | Shared contracts in `internal/api/contract`, handlers in `internal/api/core`, HTTP/UDS parity |
73	| Hook integration | Runs `tool.pre_call`, `tool.post_call`, and `tool.post_error` around registry dispatch | Hooks can deny, narrow, patch, redact, or annotate, but cannot bypass policy |
74	| Observability | Emits durable events and metrics for registration, projection, decisions, calls, failures, conflicts, truncation, and policy denials | Redacts secrets and raw tokens |
75	
76	Data flow:
77	
78	1. Extensions, built-ins, MCP servers, and future dynamic providers register cold descriptors or runtime handles.
79	2. `internal/daemon` composes providers into `internal/tools.Registry`.
80	3. Registry indexes descriptors by canonical `ToolID`.
81	4. Operator surfaces can list all tools with status and reason codes.
82	5. Session/model-visible surfaces request a session projection and receive only callable tools for that effective context.
83	6. Every invocation enters `Registry.Call`, which validates schema, recomputes availability/policy, runs hooks, calls the adapter, normalizes output, persists/observes, and returns a bounded result.

87	### Core Interfaces
88	
89	These are final-shape interface contracts for implementation planning. Implementers may add helper types, but registry dispatch must preserve these method responsibilities and must not reintroduce metadata-only runtime providers.

94		Search(ctx context.Context, scope Scope, q SearchQuery) ([]ToolView, error)

259

262	### Data-Model Field Rationale
263	
264	| Field or key | Shape | Purpose | Storage decision |
265	|---|---|---|---|
266	| `ToolID` | string, provider-safe `__`-segmented id | Single policy/dispatch/audit identity across registry, CLI, HTTP, UDS, hooks, telemetry, and hosted MCP | Typed field, never inferred from display title |
267	| `Descriptor.source` | structured `SourceRef` | Preserve raw external provenance without making prefixes the only source of truth | Typed struct; raw names are subfields, not alternate IDs |
268	| `Descriptor.input_schema` | JSON Schema object | Validate call input before dispatch | JSON schema payload is appropriate because schema content is externally structured and opaque to AGH queries |
269	| `Descriptor.output_schema` | JSON Schema object | Optional structured output contract | JSON schema payload is appropriate for the same reason as input schema |
270	| `Descriptor.visibility` | enum | Separate internal/operator/session/model surfaces | Typed enum, queryable and policy-relevant |
271	| `Descriptor.risk` | enum | Classify read/mutate/network/open-world behavior | Typed enum, never free-text metadata |
272	| `Descriptor.read_only` | bool | Drives `approve-reads` and safety checks | Typed bool; misclassification is a security bug |
273	| `Descriptor.destructive` | bool | Forces stricter policy and approval behavior | Typed bool, not metadata |
274	| `Descriptor.open_world` | bool | Marks tools that can reach arbitrary external state | Typed bool, not metadata |
275	| `Descriptor.requires_interaction` | bool | Distinguishes autonomous-safe calls from interactive calls | Typed bool, not metadata |
276	| `Descriptor.max_result_bytes` | int64 | Enforces result budget consistently across surfaces | Typed numeric field with config default |
277	| `Descriptor.toolsets` | `[]ToolsetID` | Supports recursive named bundles without overloading individual tools | Typed list; expanded to concrete `ToolID`s for lineage |
278	| `EffectiveToolDecision.reason_codes` | `[]string` enum values | Operator diagnostics and deterministic error contracts | Typed enum strings; no prose-only decisions |
279	| `[tools].enabled` | bool | Global registry execution switch | Config key with validation/defaults |
280	| `[tools].hosted_mcp_enabled` | bool | Allows session exposure through AGH-hosted MCP | Config key with validation/defaults |
281	| `[tools].default_max_result_bytes` | int64 | Default output cap when descriptor is silent | Config key with validation/defaults |
282	| `[tools.policy].external_default` | enum | Default executable policy for extension/MCP/dynamic tools | Config key, not hidden in metadata |
283	| `agent.tools` | `[]ToolID/pattern` | Allow concrete tools/patterns for an agent | Frontmatter/config field resolved to concrete lineage atoms |
284	| `agent.toolsets` | `[]ToolsetID` | Allow named bundles for an agent | Frontmatter/config field expanded before session lineage |
285	| `agent.deny_tools` | `[]ToolID/pattern` | Explicit narrowing layer for an agent | Frontmatter/config field, not runtime-only state |
286	| `extension.resources.tools.*.backend` | structured backend metadata | Connect static descriptor to MCP/sidecar/subprocess backend | Extension manifest fields, not in-process function pointers |
287	
288	No new SQLite columns are required for the MVP registry foundation. Existing session lineage stores concrete tool permission atoms; implementation should validate those atoms as canonical `ToolID`s. If a later task needs durable queryable tool-call history beyond append-only events, it must add a typed side table such as `tool_calls` rather than placing queryable call state in a session metadata JSON blob.
289	
290	### Side-Table vs JSON Decisions
291	
292	| Domain state | Decision | Rationale |
293	|---|---|---|
294	| Tool descriptors | Typed resource specs plus runtime descriptors | Descriptors are matchable by id/source/risk/visibility and must not live as opaque metadata blobs |
295	| Toolsets | Typed config/resource records | Toolset membership affects policy and lineage; it must be queryable and expandable deterministically |
296	| Tool call events | Append-only event payloads for MVP; typed side table only if queryable history is required | Events are the operational ledger; indexed call history would be matchable state and must not be hidden in JSON metadata |
297	| Source provenance | Structured `SourceRef` fields | Raw MCP/extension names are needed for debugging and collision handling |
298	| Input/output schemas | JSON Schema blobs | Schema contents are inherently opaque external contracts and are not AGH ownership state |
299	| Tool result structured payload | JSON payload plus typed envelope fields | Result body can be arbitrary, but status, bytes, truncation, redaction, and tool id are typed envelope fields |
300	| Policy decisions | Typed `EffectiveToolDecision` | Authorization is matchable and auditable; it cannot be a JSON bag |
301	| Availability reasons | Typed reason-code list | Operator diagnostics and tests need deterministic matching |

304	
305	All endpoints are implemented once in `internal/api/core` and registered by HTTP and UDS transports.

307	| Method | Path | Description |
308	|---|---|---|
309	| `GET` | `/api/tools` | List operator-visible tools with availability/policy reason codes |
310	| `POST` | `/api/tools/search` | Search tools by id, title, description, source, tags, and toolsets |
311	| `GET` | `/api/tools/{id}` | Return descriptor, availability, policy view, schema, and source provenance |
312	| `POST` | `/api/tools/{id}/invoke` | Invoke a tool through registry dispatch |
313	| `GET` | `/api/sessions/{id}/tools` | Return session/model-visible callable projection |
314	| `POST` | `/api/sessions/{id}/tools/search` | Search only within effective session-callable projection |
315	| `GET` | `/api/toolsets` | List named toolsets and expansion status |
316	| `GET` | `/api/toolsets/{id}` | Inspect one toolset expansion and conflicts |

318	Invoke request:
319	
320	```json
321	{
322	  "session_id": "sess_...",
323	  "workspace_id": "ws_...",
324	  "input": {},
325	  "approval_token": "optional-local-approval-reference"
326	}

332	{
333	  "tool_id": "agh__skill_view",

335	  "result": {},
336	  "truncated": false,

342	Status codes:
343	
344	- `200` completed/listed.
345	- `202` approval required or async dispatch accepted, only if the tool is explicitly async.
346	- `400` invalid `ToolID`, invalid schema input, or malformed request.
347	- `403` denied by ACP ceiling, session lineage, registry policy, source policy, or hook.
348	- `404` not found or hidden from caller context.
349	- `409` conflicted canonical ID or sanitized external name.
350	- `422` registered but unavailable or not executable.
351	- `500` internal daemon error.

364	## Integration Points
365	
366	### ACP
367	
368	ACP does not impose a callable tool registry pattern. AGH must not use ACP `ToolCall.title` as a policy identity or dispatch key.
369	
370	AGH will integrate with ACP by:
371	
372	- passing the hosted AGH MCP server in ACP `mcpServers` during session creation/load when the selected agent supports MCP;
373	- preserving ACP tool-call observations as session events keyed by `toolCallId`;
374	- mapping AGH-owned tool calls back into ACP lifecycle updates where relevant;
375	- treating ACP `ToolKind` as risk/display metadata, not identity;
376	- keeping `permissions.mode` as the system/session approval ceiling.
377	
378	### Hosted MCP
379	
380	MVP exposure path:
381	
382	```text
383	agent session -> ACP mcpServers -> agh tool mcp --session <id> -> UDS -> daemon Tool Registry
384	```
385	
386	The hosted MCP server lists only session-callable tools. It exposes MCP `Tool.name` equal to AGH canonical `ToolID`. It does not expose unavailable, unauthorized, or conflicted tools to the model-visible surface.
387	
388	### Extensions
389	
390	Extension-installed tools are possible and should be first-class.
391	
392	Current foundation already has:
393	
394	- `extension.toml` `resources.tools`
395	- `resources.publish.families = ["tools"]`
396	- resource projection into daemon tool records
397	- extension health/status infrastructure
398	- Host API capability checks
399	
400	This TechSpec extends that by adding backend metadata and runtime projection:
401	
402	```toml
403	[resources.tools.search]
404	description = "Search Linear issues"
405	read_only = true
406	backend.kind = "mcp"
407	backend.server = "linear"
408	backend.tool = "search"
409	toolsets = ["linear__read"]
410	```
411	
412	Canonical ID is derived by the registry from the trusted source context, for example:
413	
414	```text
415	ext__linear__search
416	```
417	
418	Extensions must not freely impersonate `agh__*` or another extension namespace. Raw manifest names remain in `SourceRef`.
419	
420	Execution boundaries:
421	
422	- `mcp`: call through MCP client/adapter.
423	- `extension_host`: call the running extension sidecar through a typed Host API/protocol method.
424	- `subprocess`: launch a bounded subprocess through AGH-owned process supervision.
425	- `bridge`: future bridge adapter.
426	- `builtin`: reserved for AGH-owned daemon code only.
427	
428	No in-process third-party extension handlers in MVP.
429	
430	### MCP Sources
431	
432	MCP-backed tools use:
433	
434	```text
435	mcp__<server>__<tool>
436	```
437	
438	The registry must preserve raw server/tool names in `SourceRef`. Sanitization collisions fail closed and mark the candidate tool `conflicted`.
439	
440	AGH-managed MCP sources come first in MVP. Client-supplied ACP `mcpServers` can be a later compatibility extension only if they become session-scoped tool sources with explicit source trust and collision handling.
441	
442	### Hooks
443	
444	Existing tool hook concepts become part of central registry dispatch:
445	
446	- `tool.pre_call`
447	- `tool.post_call`
448	- `tool.post_error`
449	
450	Payloads should use canonical `tool_id`. Existing `tool_name` / `tool_namespace` usage should be replaced in the registry path to avoid dual identity.
451	
452	### Skills
453	
454	Built-in skill tools prove progressive disclosure:
455	
456	- `agh__skill_list`
457	- `agh__skill_search`
458	- `agh__skill_view`
459	
460	These call into `internal/skills.Registry`, respect workspace overlays, reuse content verification, and enforce result budgets. Install/remove/update tools are intentionally out of MVP unless supply-chain policy/scanning is expanded.

463	
464	MVP includes:
465	
466	- `agh__network_peers`
467	- `agh__network_send`
468	- bounded `agh__task_*` tools

470	These must route through existing network/task services and existing authorization rules. Mutating tools must not be classified as read-only.

590	
591	### Global `config.toml`
592	
593	Add:
594	
595	```toml
596	[tools]
597	enabled = true
598	hosted_mcp_enabled = true
599	default_max_result_bytes = 262144
600	
601	[tools.policy]
602	external_default = "disabled"
603	```
604	
605	Semantics:
606	
607	- `tools.enabled=false` disables AGH-owned registry dispatch and hosted MCP exposure, but operator diagnostics can still show static resources where safe.
608	- `hosted_mcp_enabled=true` allows AGH to inject/offer the local hosted MCP proxy for sessions.
609	- `default_max_result_bytes` applies when a descriptor does not specify a smaller limit.
610	- `external_default="disabled"` means extension/MCP/dynamic executable tools are registered and operator-visible, but not session-callable until enabled by explicit policy/toolset grants. Built-in AGH bootstrap tools remain enabled by default subject to ACP/session policy.
611	
612	Allowed `external_default` values:
613	
614	- `disabled`
615	- `ask`
616	- `enabled`
617	
618	MVP default is `disabled`.

747	### Technical Dependencies
748	
749	Blocking dependencies that must be resolved before implementation:
750	
751	- Existing ACP `permissions.mode` behavior in `internal/acp/permission.go`.
752	- Existing session lineage permission atoms in `internal/store/session_lineage.go`.
753	- Existing extension resource publication and `resources.publish.families = ["tools"]`.
754	- Existing hooks payload system.
755	- Existing MCP server configuration/resource lifecycle.
756	- Existing skills registry APIs.
757	- Existing task/network services.
758	- OpenAPI/codegen path for web contract updates.
759	
760	### Safety Invariants
761	
762	1. Every AGH-owned tool call enters `internal/tools.Registry.Call`; CLI, HTTP, UDS, hosted MCP, extension, and session paths cannot bypass the registry dispatch pipeline.
763	2. Dispatch recomputes availability and `EffectiveToolDecision` at call time, even when discovery already filtered the tool.
764	3. ACP `permissions.mode` is always a ceiling. Registry, source, agent, session, and hook policy can narrow authority but cannot raise it above the effective ACP/session mode.
765	4. `approve-all` skips approval prompts only for otherwise allowed tools; it does not bypass explicit denies, source grants, session lineage, conflicts, unavailable backends, or hooks.
766	5. `approve-reads` applies only to registry-classified read-only tools. Mutating, destructive, open-world, network-send, and task-write tools cannot inherit read approval by display title or ACP kind.
767	6. Session lineage stores concrete canonical `ToolID` atoms after toolset expansion; child sessions can only receive a subset of parent concrete atoms.
768	7. Tool ID collisions fail closed. A conflicted tool is operator-visible with reason codes and absent from session/model-visible projections.
769	8. Extension-installed tools are descriptor-only until an out-of-process backend handle is healthy, available, authorized, and policy-allowed.
770	9. Third-party extension tool handlers never run in-process in the daemon during MVP.
771	10. Hooks dispatch at the call site and cannot tail event tables, spawn parallel queues, or mutate durable ownership state outside typed hook contracts.
772	11. Tool result limiting and redaction run before results cross CLI, HTTP, UDS, MCP, SSE, logs, memory, or event payloads.
773	12. Raw `claim_token`, MCP auth tokens, OAuth codes, PKCE verifiers, and secret bindings never appear in tool inputs/outputs persisted or emitted by AGH-owned surfaces.
774	13. Hosted MCP `tools/list` is a projection of `GET /api/sessions/{id}/tools`; divergence is a test failure.
775	14. Operator-visible diagnostics are not security boundaries. Hidden session projection plus dispatch-time revalidation is the security boundary.
776	15. Toolsets are expanded deterministically and cycle-checked before session projection; recursive expansion cannot happen lazily during dispatch.

837	## Technical Considerations
838	
839	### Key Decisions
840	
841	Decision: Tool Registry is an AGH daemon runtime service, not an ACP registry.
842	
843	Rationale: ACP has no callable tool registry and relies on MCP for tool discovery.
844	
845	Trade-off: AGH must maintain its own registry semantics and expose them through MCP/session surfaces.
846	
847	Decision: Use one canonical `ToolID` with `__` namespace separators.
848	
849	Rationale: Avoid dotted/internal plus wire alias ambiguity and stay compatible with stricter provider naming limits.
850	
851	Trade-off: `agh__skill_view` is less visually elegant than `agh.skill.view`, but it prevents dual identity bugs.
852	
853	Decision: Built-ins can execute in-process; extension tools execute out-of-process.
854	
855	Rationale: Daemon safety and extension isolation matter more than plugin convenience.
856	
857	Trade-off: Extension tool latency and setup are higher, but failure containment is better.
858	
859	Decision: ACP `permissions.mode` is the approval ceiling.
860	
861	Rationale: Avoid contradictory policy layers and keep existing settings truthful.
862	
863	Trade-off: Registry policy must compute effective decisions rather than simple allow/deny flags.
864	
865	Decision: Operator and session projections differ.
866	
867	Rationale: Operators need diagnostics; models should see only callable tools.
868	
869	Trade-off: More projection logic, but less model confusion and fewer unsafe calls.
870	
871	Decision: Keep cold resource records separate from runtime handles.
872	
873	Rationale: Resource/projector system is good for installed metadata; executable dispatch needs function/protocol handles and live health.
874	
875	Trade-off: More types, but cleaner lifecycle and safer extension support.
876	
877	### Known Risks
878	
879	Risk: Mutating tools mislabeled as read-only.
880	
881	Mitigation: Descriptor validation, review tests, policy matrix tests, and hook/audit visibility.
882	
883	Risk: External tool name collisions force late breaking changes.
884	
885	Mitigation: Enforce canonical `ToolID` grammar and fail-closed collision handling in MVP.
886	
887	Risk: Hosted MCP path diverges from CLI/HTTP/UDS behavior.
888	
889	Mitigation: Hosted MCP proxies through UDS into the same registry dispatch path.
890	
891	Risk: Extension tools become visible before safe backend execution exists.
892	
893	Mitigation: Operator-visible only until availability, source policy, and backend handle all pass.
894	
895	Risk: `approve-all` is misunderstood as "execute everything."
896	
897	Mitigation: Docs and UI copy must clarify it auto-approves otherwise allowed calls; it does not bypass explicit denies, source grants, availability, lineage, or hooks.
898	
899	Risk: Toolsets create ambiguous policy language.
900	
901	Mitigation: Keep `tools` and `toolsets` as separate typed fields; expand toolsets to concrete `ToolID`s for session lineage.
902	
903	Risk: Result payloads leak secrets or overwhelm context.
904	
905	Mitigation: Central result limiter, redaction metadata, digesting, and output budgets.
906	
907	### Delete Targets
908	
909	Because AGH is greenfield alpha, the implementation should hard-cut ambiguous old tool concepts instead of adding compatibility bridges:
910	
911	- Replace metadata-only `ToolProvider.Tools(ctx)` as the runtime extension point with provider/descriptor/handle contracts.
912	- Replace `internal/tools.Tool.Name` as a registry identity with canonical `ToolID` in new public contracts.
913	- Remove any new public use of dotted tool IDs or separate MCP wire aliases.
914	- Replace hook policy identity based on `tool_name` + `tool_namespace` with canonical `tool_id` for registry-owned tool calls.
915	- Update docs, tests, CLI/API examples, and task artifacts that refer to dotted IDs or dual aliasing.

1	# AGH Current State: Tool Registry Foundation
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	AGH already has the cold side of a tool catalog, but not the runtime side.
6	`internal/tools` defines a canonical `tool` resource shape and the daemon projects tool records through the generic resources system. Extensions can publish static tool metadata from `extension.toml`. Sessions can also carry concrete permission atoms for tools in lineage metadata.
7	
8	What is missing is the executable registry: a central service that can answer which tools exist for a specific agent/session, whether each tool is available now, whether the caller is allowed to use it, and how to dispatch the call through one uniform pipeline.
9	
10	This matters because AGH's product premise is agent-first manageability. A tool registry is incomplete if it only helps internal Go code list metadata or if each ACP runtime owns a private tool universe that AGH cannot inspect, govern, or extend.
11	
12	## Existing Mechanisms
13	
14	### Tool resource metadata
15	
16	`internal/tools/tool.go` defines `ToolSource` values for `builtin`, `mcp`, `extension`, and `dynamic`, plus a small `Tool` record:
17	
18	- `Name`
19	- `Description`
20	- `InputSchema`
21	- `ReadOnly`
22	- `Source`
23	
24	The only provider interface is:
25	
26	```go
27	type ToolProvider interface {
28	    Tools(ctx context.Context) ([]Tool, error)
29	}
30	```
31	
32	There is no `Call`, `Availability`, `CheckPermission`, `Aliases`, `Namespace`, `IsConcurrencySafe`, `IsDestructive`, `MaxResultBytes`, `Owner`, `Visibility`, or provenance-rich source metadata.
33	
34	### Desired-state resources
35	
36	`internal/tools/resource.go` defines `ToolResourceKind = "tool"` and validates tool records as JSON-object specs with a maximum size of 256 KiB. This is a good base for persisted inventory, desired-state reconciliation, and extension-published tool metadata.
37	
38	The daemon already has a generic `resourceCatalog[T]` in `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go` and a `newToolProjector` that projects reconciled `tool` records into a daemon-local snapshot. This catalog is descriptive and revisioned, but it is not executable.
39	
40	### Extension-published tools
41	
42	`internal/extension/manifest.go` already lets extensions declare:
43	
44	```go
45	type ResourcesConfig struct {
46	    Tools map[string]ToolConfig `toml:"tools,omitempty" json:"tools,omitempty"`
47	    MCPServers map[string]MCPServerConfig `toml:"mcp_servers,omitempty" json:"mcp_servers,omitempty"`
48	}
49	```
50	
51	`ToolConfig` carries description, input schema, and read-only status. `ResolveManifestToolResources` converts these manifest entries into `toolspkg.Tool` records with `Source = ToolSourceExtension`. The daemon syncer publishes them into the resource graph with source keys like `extension/<name>/tool/<tool>`.
52	
53	This is close to OpenClaw's manifest-first model, but AGH currently stops at metadata. A manifest-declared extension tool is not callable unless some separate ACP/MCP/runtime surface happens to expose it.
54	
55	### MCP resources
56	
57	The same extension manifest can declare MCP servers, and the daemon sync path resolves them into desired-state MCP server resources. Skills can also declare MCP sidecars. This gives AGH a strong candidate adapter for extension tools: manifest tools may be backed by an MCP server, an extension sidecar Host API endpoint, or native AGH built-ins, but all should normalize into one registry contract.
58	
59	### Session permission atoms
60	
61	`internal/store/session_lineage.go` defines `SessionPermissionPolicy` with concrete atoms:
62	
63	- `Tools`
64	- `Skills`
65	- `MCPServers`
66	- `WorkspacePaths`
67	- `NetworkChannels`
68	- `SandboxProfiles`
69	
70	`internal/session/spawn.go` validates child permissions as a subset of parent permissions. This is an important base for runtime tool policy because it is already persisted with session lineage and already participates in spawn delegation.
71	
72	The current agent definition has a flat `Tools []string` field in `internal/config/agent.go`. It lacks allow/deny overlays, named toolsets, namespace patterns, visibility tiers, and risk classes.
73	
74	### Hooks around tool calls
75	
76	`internal/hooks/payloads.go` already defines `ToolPreCallPayload`, `ToolPostCallPayload`, `ToolPostErrorPayload`, `ToolCallPatch`, and `ToolResultPatch`. This is the right policy extension point for a centralized dispatch pipeline:
77	
78	- pre-call hooks can deny or mutate input
79	- post-call hooks can redact or mutate output
80	- post-error hooks can classify or recover failures
81	
82	The current gap is that AGH does not have a single dispatch pipeline that all AGH-owned tools must pass through.
83	
84	### Skills registry contrast
85	
86	`internal/skills.Registry` is much more mature than tools. It has global snapshots, workspace overlays, content loading, verification, install provenance, and `GlobalVersion()` for invalidation. Skills are injected as a static prompt catalog at session start, while tool resources are not exposed as a session-callable registry.
87	
88	The Tool Registry should copy the skills registry's useful properties where they fit: global/workspace overlays, versioned snapshots, resource projection, progressive disclosure, and explicit content/schema loading.
89	
90	## Gaps
91	
92	1. No central runtime registry that owns executable tool handles.
93	2. No agent-facing discovery API for tools.
94	3. No native AGH tools such as `agh__tool_search`, `agh__skill_view`, or `agh__network_send`.
95	4. No availability model for env vars, binaries, MCP health, extension health, workspace scope, or policy state.
96	5. No central permission pipeline for AGH-owned tools.
97	6. No namespace or structured provenance model, so duplicate names would be ambiguous.
98	7. No toolsets or bundles comparable to skills/capabilities.
99	8. No direct extension execution boundary for manifest-declared tools.
100	9. No consistent way to expose tools over CLI, HTTP, UDS, and session-visible agent surfaces.
101	10. No usage telemetry by tool or skill.
102	11. No result-size budget, persistence policy, redaction, or output mapping at registry level.
103	12. The `dynamic` source enum exists but has no producer.
104

118	- `internal/skills/registry.go:100-103`: skills expose a global version suitable for catalog delta detection.
119	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/analysis/analysis_skills_tools_registry.md`: prior autonomy gap analysis with G1-G12 and P1-P9 proposals.
120	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/analysis/analysis_tools_security.md`: security gaps relevant once AGH exposes agent-callable tools.
121	
122	## Design Constraints for the TechSpec
123	
124	The Tool Registry should be a foundation, not a pile of built-in commands. It should define the contracts, policy path, extension boundary, and surfaces first, then add a small bootstrap set of native AGH tools to prove the system.
125	
126	AGH should avoid copying in-process plugin patterns from Python/TypeScript systems. Third-party executable tools should cross a process/protocol boundary: MCP, extension sidecar Host API, subprocess adapter, or future bridge SDK. Built-in Go tools can register in-process because they are part of the daemon binary.
127	
128	The cold `tool` resource should remain valuable as catalog metadata and desired state, but executable dispatch must be modeled separately. A manifest-declared tool can be installed and discoverable while still being unavailable until its backend is healthy and authorized.
129	
130	Availability and authorization must both be rechecked at dispatch time. Hiding unavailable tools from discovery is useful, but it is not a security boundary.
131	
132	## Open Questions
133	
134	1. Should extension tools be executable in the MVP, or should MVP only make them discoverable with an explicit unavailable state?
135	2. If executable, should extension tools be allowed only through MCP/sidecar boundaries, or should trusted bundled extensions get in-process handlers?
136	3. Should the first AGH-native tool surface be injected into ACP sessions, exposed as an MCP server hosted by AGH, or exposed only through CLI/UDS/HTTP for drivers to call indirectly?
137	4. Should `internal/catalog` coordinate tools and skills, or should `internal/tools` own runtime tools while a thinner catalog/search service composes skills and tools?
138	5. Which visibility tiers are needed for MVP: internal, CLI/HTTP, agent-visible, model-visible, deferred-discoverable, extension-private?
139	
140	## Evidence
141	
142	- `internal/tools/tool.go:91-136`: current `Tool` and `ToolProvider` are descriptive and list-only.
143	- `internal/tools/resource.go:13-61`: tool resource codec validates metadata records.
144	- `internal/extension/manifest.go:55-62`: extension manifests can publish `resources.tools`.
145	- `internal/extension/resource_publication.go:13-31`: extension manifest tools become static tool resources.

1	# Tool Registry Synthesis and Proposed Direction
2	
3	## Executive Summary
4	
5	AGH should build a Tool Registry as a daemon-owned runtime service that composes tool metadata, availability, policy, execution, hooks, telemetry, and extension adapters.
6	
7	The foundation should not be "add many built-in tools." The foundation should be:
8	
9	1. a first-class runtime tool contract,
10	2. a registry that aggregates built-ins, MCP, extensions, and dynamic providers,
11	3. a policy/availability projection for each agent/session,
12	4. a single dispatch pipeline,
13	5. an extension-safe execution boundary,
14	6. agent-manageable CLI/HTTP/UDS/session surfaces,
15	7. toolsets/bundles comparable to skills.
16	
17	The ACP inventory found `.resources/openfang` present, but with no meaningful ACP evidence.
18	
19	## Recommended Architecture
20	
21	### Accepted decisions so far
22	
23	- Extension tool execution boundary: manifest-first descriptors with out-of-process execution only for extension tools in the MVP.
24	- Session exposure path: AGH-hosted local MCP server plus shared CLI/HTTP/UDS contracts.
25	- Package boundary: `internal/tools` owns runtime registry contracts and dispatch; a thin `internal/catalog` facade composes tools and skills for cross-domain discovery.
26	- MVP native tool scope: bootstrap catalog/skill tools plus network and bounded task tools (`agh__tool_*`, `agh__skill_*`, `agh__network_peers`, `agh__network_send`, `agh__task_*`).
27	- Policy integration: existing ACP `permissions.mode` is the system/session approval ceiling; registry policy is a granular layer below it and cannot silently grant more authority than ACP policy allows.
28	- Visibility by surface: operator surfaces show unavailable/unauthorized/conflicted tools with reason codes; session/model-visible surfaces expose only tools callable in that effective session context.
29	- Naming/collision policy: one canonical public `ToolID` uses provider-safe lower snake segments separated by reserved `__`, for example `agh__skill_view` and `mcp__github__create_issue`; this is captured in ADR-007.
30	
31	### ACP compatibility finding
32	
33	ACP does not define a durable callable-tool registry. It defines session lifecycle, `mcpServers` bootstrap fields, client authority callbacks, permission requests, and observable tool-call events. ACP `ToolCall` records have `toolCallId`, human-readable `title`, coarse `kind`, status, locations, raw input/output, and content, but no programmatic tool `name` equivalent to MCP `Tool.name`.
34	
35	This means the Tool Registry should remain an AGH daemon/runtime service. Session exposure should use the accepted AGH-hosted MCP path, where AGH exposes the canonical `ToolID` directly as the hosted MCP `Tool.name`.
36	
37	Accepted identity format:
38	
39	- `ToolID`: stable provider-safe id with reserved `__` namespace separators, such as `agh__skill_view`.
40	- `DisplayTitle`: human-readable and non-unique.
41	- `SourceRef`: structured provenance, not inferred only from prefixes.
42	
43	Collision handling must be fail-closed. Canonical ID collisions are provider registration errors or operator diagnostics. Sanitized external-name collisions make the affected tools unavailable to that session until resolved. Display title collisions are allowed because titles are not policy identities.
44	
45	### 1. Split descriptor, runtime handle, and resource record
46	
47	Keep the existing `internal/tools.Tool` resource shape as the cold catalog/desired-state record, but introduce a runtime contract with separate types:
48	
49	- `ToolID`: stable provider-safe id such as `agh__skill_view`, `mcp__github__create_issue`, `ext__linear__search`.
50	- `Descriptor`: identity, description, input schema, optional output schema, read-only/destructive/open-world/concurrency metadata, source/provenance, visibility, tags, owner, result budget.
51	- `Handle`: descriptor plus `Availability(ctx, ToolContext)` and `Call(ctx, ToolCall)` for executable tools.
52	- `Provider`: contributes descriptors/handles and can refresh.
53	- `Registry`: owns provider registration, indexing, listing, search, policy projection, and dispatch.
54	- `ToolResult`: structured output, preview, artifacts, redactions, bytes, display title, metadata.
55	
56	This avoids overloading the desired-state resource with function pointers while still allowing resource records to feed the runtime registry.
57	
58	### 2. Use manifest-first extension tools
59	
60	Extension manifests should continue to declare tool metadata statically. Add enough metadata to connect the declaration to a backend:
61	
62	- backend kind: `mcp`, `extension_host`, `subprocess`, or `builtin` where appropriate;
63	- namespace/owner;
64	- visibility;
65	- risk class;
66	- required config/env/capabilities;
67	- optional toolset memberships.
68	
69	The registry can list these tools without executing extension code. A tool becomes executable only when its backend adapter is healthy, authorized, and has a callable handle.
70	
71	Recommendation for MVP: no in-process third-party handlers. Built-in Go tools can register in-process. Extension tools should execute through MCP or an extension sidecar/Host API adapter.
72	
73	### 3. Make availability a state machine, not a boolean
74	
75	Use explicit status:
76	
77	- `registered`: descriptor exists.
78	- `enabled`: operator/session policy has not disabled it.
79	- `available`: dependencies are present and backend is healthy.
80	- `authorized`: caller policy permits visibility/use.
81	- `executable`: there is a live handle for dispatch.
82	- `conflicted`: id/name collision requires resolution.
83	
84	Discovery can hide unavailable/unauthorized tools from agents while operator surfaces show reasons. Dispatch must recheck availability and authorization.
85	
86	The registry should expose separate operator and session projections. The operator projection includes diagnostics, source/provenance, policy reasons, availability reasons, and conflicts. The session projection powers hosted MCP and future driver injection and includes only tools that pass effective visibility/execution gates for that session.
87	
88	### 4. Centralize dispatch
89	
90	Every AGH-owned tool call should pass through:
91	
92	1. resolve tool id/alias in context,
93	2. validate input against schema,
94	3. compute availability,
95	4. evaluate policy and session permission atoms,
96	5. run `tool.pre_call` hooks,
97	6. enforce concurrency/rate/result budgets,
98	7. call provider adapter,
99	8. normalize result,
100	9. redact/truncate/persist output,
101	10. run `tool.post_call` or `tool.post_error` hooks,
102	11. emit telemetry.
103	
104	No CLI, HTTP, UDS, MCP, extension, or session path should bypass this pipeline.
105	
106	### 5. Model policy as overlays
107	
108	Use one policy engine that combines:
109	
110	- system/session ACP `permissions.mode`,
111	- daemon defaults,
112	- workspace config,
113	- extension grants,
114	- agent definition,
115	- session lineage `SessionPermissionPolicy.Tools`,
116	- skill/command scoped grants where relevant,
117	- explicit allow/deny patterns,
118	- named toolsets,
119	- risk defaults.
120	
121	Toolsets should be recursive resources/config entries. This copies Hermes' strongest idea while fitting AGH's resource model.
122	
123	The registry must not create a second approval system that contradicts ACP. `approve-all` removes automatic approval prompts for otherwise allowed tools, but explicit registry denies, source grants, session lineage restrictions, availability failures, and hooks still apply. `approve-reads` auto-approves only registry-classified read-only tools. `deny-all` denies execution by default and requires an explicit approval path.
124	
125	### 6. Provide a small bootstrap native toolset
126	
127	The TechSpec should not enumerate every future AGH tool. It should require a small proving set:
128	
129	- `agh__tool_list`
130	- `agh__tool_search`
131	- `agh__tool_info`
132	- `agh__skill_list`
133	- `agh__skill_view`
134	
135	Optional later groups:
136	
137	- `agh__skill_install`
138	- `agh__network_peers`
139	- `agh__network_send`
140	- `agh__task_*`
141	- `agh__extension_*`
142	
143	The bootstrap set proves discovery, schema loading, skill body loading, policy, result budget, and telemetry without overcommitting the whole daemon.
144	
145	### 7. Expose agent-manageable surfaces
146	
147	The registry should have shared contract types used by:
148	
149	- CLI: `agh tool list/search/info/invoke`.
150	- HTTP: `/api/tools`, `/api/tools/{id}`, `/api/tools/{id}/invoke`.
151	- UDS: same operations for local agents and internal tools.
152	- Session-visible tool surface: either an AGH-hosted MCP server, driver-specific ACP tool injection where possible, or a fallback where agents can use `agh` CLI/UDS through their runtime.
153	
154	The TechSpec should pick one MVP path and keep the others as contract-compatible surfaces.
155	
156	### 8. Treat Tool Search as provider-neutral
157	
158	Claude Code's `tool_reference` mechanism is useful but not portable. AGH should implement registry search as a normal catalog operation first:
159	
160	- search over name, namespace, description, tags, source, toolset, and search hints;
161	- return metadata first;
162	- load schema/details on demand;
163	- optionally persist discovered state per session later.
164	
165	Driver-specific schema-on-demand integration can be a future enhancement.
166	
167	### 9. Reuse existing AGH infrastructure
168	
169	Build on:
170	
171	- `internal/tools` for contracts and registry,
172	- `internal/resources` for desired-state records,
173	- `internal/extension` manifest publication,
174	- `internal/hooks` for pre/post/error dispatch gates,
175	- `internal/store.SessionPermissionPolicy` for lineage constraints,
176	- `internal/skills.Registry` for skill listing/content,
177	- `internal/toolruntime` for subprocess ownership if extension tools need process handles,
178	- `internal/api/contract` for shared HTTP/UDS payloads.
179	
180	Avoid a large generic `internal/catalog` at first unless it only coordinates cross-domain search. The runtime tool registry belongs in or near `internal/tools`; a catalog facade can compose tools and skills for `agh__tool_*` / `agh__skill_*`.
181	
182	## Proposed MVP Scope
183	
184	### In scope
185	
186	- Runtime tool registry contract and central dispatch pipeline.
187	- Built-in provider for `agh__tool_list`, `agh__tool_search`, `agh__tool_info`, `agh__skill_list`, `agh__skill_search`, `agh__skill_view`, `agh__network_peers`, `agh__network_send`, and a bounded `agh__task_*` set.
188	- Resource-backed descriptors from existing `tool` records.
189	- Extension manifest backend metadata for future executable extension tools.
190	- MCP adapter design, even if full MCP call-through is deferred.
191	- Context-specific list/search/info APIs.
192	- Tool policy with allow/deny patterns and named toolsets.
193	- Availability model and reason codes.
194	- Hook integration for pre/post/error.
195	- Telemetry events for list/search/info/call and failures.
196	- CLI/HTTP/UDS contract surfaces.
197	
198	### Out of scope for MVP
199	
200	- Full shell/browser/file tool replacement for ACP runtimes.
201	- Provider-specific Anthropic `tool_reference` integration.
202	- In-process third-party extension handlers.
203	- Large catalog of AGH-native tools beyond the selected catalog/skill/network/task set.
204	- Skill install/remove/update tools unless explicitly paired with supply-chain policy/scanning work.
205	- Network peer remote tool execution.
206	- Marketplace signing/trust overhaul, except for explicit risk hooks needed by extension tools.
207	
208	## Critical Decisions Before TechSpec
209	
210	1. Extension execution boundary: out-of-process only, metadata-only first, or trusted in-process handlers.
211	2. Session exposure path: hosted MCP, direct ACP extension, CLI/UDS fallback, or all in phases.
212	3. Package boundary: runtime registry in `internal/tools` with a catalog facade, or a new `internal/catalog` owning tools and skills together.
213	4. MVP tool set: only list/search/info/view, or include mutating install/network/task tools.
214	5. Policy defaults: external tools disabled, ask, or visible-but-not-callable until granted.
215	6. Availability visibility: hide unavailable tools from agents, show unavailable tools with reasons, or configurable by surface.
216	7. Naming/collision policy: accepted in ADR-007. Use one canonical provider-safe `ToolID` with reserved `__` namespace separators, display-only title, structured provenance, and no shadowing or silent sanitized-name collisions.
217	
218	## Competitor Pattern Matrix
219	
220	| Pattern | Hermes | Claude Code | GoClaw | OpenClaw | AGH Recommendation |
221	|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
222	| Single normalized tool contract | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Required |
223	| Runtime executable registry | Yes | Distributed | Yes | Yes | Required |
224	| Manifest-first extension discovery | Partial | Plugin metadata | Partial | Strong | Required |
225	| MCP as adapter | Yes | Strong | Yes | Strong | Required |
226	| Availability gating | Strong discovery | `isEnabled` + MCP state | Policy/lazy checks | Lifecycle state | Required at discovery and dispatch |
227	| Central dispatch | Mostly | Strong | Mostly | Gateway + adapters | Required with no bypass |
228	| Toolsets/bundles | Strong | Policy lists | Groups | Policy groups | Required |
229	| Deferred search | Partial | Strong | Search helpers | Partial | Provider-neutral MVP |
230	| Concurrency metadata | Partial | Strong | Partial | Partial | Required metadata, scheduling can evolve |
231	| Extension in-process handlers | Yes | No native direct tools | Some | Plugin API | Avoid for MVP |
232	
233	## Implementation Shape to Explore in TechSpec
234	
235	```go

275	
276	## Risks
277	
278	If AGH exposes tool invocation before policy and availability are in place, it will create a broader attack surface than today's ACP-delegated tools.
279	
280	If extension tools can run in-process, one bad extension can compromise the daemon.
281	
282	If the registry only lists resources but does not dispatch, AGH will still lack the "last mile" that motivated the feature.
283	
284	If the registry only works through one surface, agents will not be able to manage it consistently.
285	
286	If name collisions are postponed, extension/MCP adoption will force a breaking change later.
