1	# Analysis: ACP Tool Registry Compatibility
2	
3	## Scope
4	
5	This analysis answers whether ACP imposes a tool registry pattern that AGH must follow, and which `.resources/*` projects materially use ACP in ways that affect the Tool Registry TechSpec. The research combines official ACP/MCP documentation with read-only subagent passes over `.resources/rayclaw`, `.resources/harnss`, `.resources/acpx`, `.resources/openclaw`, `.resources/opencode`, and an inventory pass across every top-level `.resources/*` project.
6	
7	## Executive Conclusion
8	
9	ACP does not define a durable, programmatic tool registry for callable tools. ACP defines session lifecycle, prompt streaming, client authority callbacks, permission requests, MCP server bootstrap fields, and observable tool-call events. Those tool-call events carry `toolCallId`, `title`, `kind`, `status`, locations, raw input, raw output, and content, but they do not carry a stable `name` field equivalent to MCP `Tool.name`.
10	
11	Therefore, AGH should not model its Tool Registry as an ACP registry, and should not use ACP `title` as a durable policy or collision key.
12	
13	For session-visible AGH tools, the strongest compatibility path remains the accepted MVP path: an AGH-hosted MCP server backed by the daemon Tool Registry. MCP supplies the externally callable `Tool.name`; ACP supplies the way ACP-compatible agents receive `mcpServers`, report tool execution, and request permission.
14	
15	The practical design correction is:
16	
17	- AGH should use one canonical provider-safe `ToolID` everywhere, using reserved double-underscore namespace separators, for example `agh__skill_view`, `mcp__github__create_issue`, or `ext__linear__search`.
18	- The same `ToolID` should be the hosted MCP `Tool.name`; AGH should not introduce a second wire alias in the MVP.
19	- ACP `title` is display-only and event-only.
20	- ACP `ToolKind` is a risk/display hint, not registry identity.
21	- `permissions.mode` remains the session approval ceiling; registry policy remains the granular layer below it.
22	
23	## Official Protocol Constraints
24	
25	### ACP
26	
27	Official ACP schema evidence:
28	
29	- ACP `session/new`, `session/load`, and `session/resume` include `mcpServers`; agents are expected to connect to those MCP servers for the session. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>.
30	- ACP `ToolCall` is event/reporting data with `toolCallId`, `title`, `kind`, `status`, `rawInput`, `rawOutput`, `locations`, and `content`. The schema describes `title` as human-readable and `toolCallId` as unique within a session. It does not expose a durable callable `name` field. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>.
31	- ACP `ToolKind` values are coarse categories such as `read`, `edit`, `delete`, `move`, `search`, `execute`, `think`, `fetch`, `switch_mode`, and `other`. The schema says these help clients pick icons and display progress, which is weaker than registry identity. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>.
32	- ACP `session/request_permission` carries a `toolCall` object plus permission options. It is a permission bridge for a concrete tool call, not a registry discovery API. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>.
33	- The official "ACP Registry" is an agent registry: a catalog of ACP-compatible agents and their install/run metadata, not a callable tool registry. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/registry>.
34	
35	### MCP
36	
37	Official MCP schema evidence:
38	
39	- MCP `tools/list` returns `Tool[]`.
40	- MCP `Tool` has `name`, optional `title`, optional `description`, `inputSchema`, optional `outputSchema`, annotations, execution metadata, and `_meta`.
41	- MCP describes `name` as intended for programmatic/logical use and `title` as intended for UI/end-user contexts. Source: <https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/draft/schema>.
42	
43	Implication: AGH should treat MCP `Tool.name` as the session wire name when exposing AGH registry tools through hosted MCP. ACP does not replace that name.
44	
45	## ACP Usage Inventory Across `.resources/*`
46	
47	| Project | ACP usage | Tool registry relevance |
48	|---|---|---|
49	| `.resources/acpx` | ACP client/orchestrator and conformance tooling. | Has an agent/adapter registry, not a callable tool registry. Passes `mcpServers`; models tool calls by `toolCallId`, title, kind, status, raw input/output. |
50	| `.resources/collaborator-ai` | ACP client/orchestrator. | No registry found. Uses ACP tool update titles for display. |
51	| `.resources/goclaw` | ACP client/orchestrator. | No formal registry. Uses method switches and permission heuristics for ACP callbacks. |
52	| `.resources/harnss` | ACP client/orchestrator with Electron bridge. | Has ACP agent registry and UI rendering adapters, not a tool registry. Converts configured MCP servers to ACP `McpServer[]`. |
53	| `.resources/hermes` | ACP server/agent implementation plus ACP client shim. | Relevant: registers ACP-provided MCP servers into Hermes agent state and valid tool names; maps Hermes tools to ACP `ToolKind` and titles. |
54	| `.resources/multica` | ACP client/orchestrator. | No formal registry. Parses titles such as `terminal:` and `read:` for UI normalization. |
55	| `.resources/openclaw` | ACP server/client/runtime bridge. | Has internal tool catalog and plugin/MCP surfaces, but main ACP bridge does not expose an ACP tool registry and rejects per-session `mcpServers`. |
56	| `.resources/opencode` | Native ACP server. | Has a real internal `ToolRegistry`, but ACP does not expose it as a registry API. Accepts ACP `mcpServers` and converts them into internal MCP config. |
57	| `.resources/paperclip` | Docs/reference only. | Conceptual ACP references only. |
58	| `.resources/rayclaw` | ACP client/orchestrator. | Exposes ACP control as local `acp_*` tools; ACP-reported tool calls are telemetry, not registry entries. |
59	| `.resources/sandbox-agent` | ACP adapter/proxy/client package. | Agent launch registry only; no ACP tool registry found. |
60	| `.resources/t3code` | ACP schema/client/runtime package. | Schema and runtime tracking for ACP tool events; no broad tool registry. |
61	
62	No meaningful ACP evidence was found in `.resources/cc-posts`, `.resources/chat`, `.resources/openfang`, `.resources/pi`, or `.resources/symphony`. `.resources/claude-code` had an `ACP` false positive inside an embedded/base64-like string, not implementation evidence.
63	
64	## Deep Dives
65	
66	### RayClaw
67	
68	RayClaw is an ACP client/orchestrator. It spawns configured ACP agents, runs JSON-RPC lifecycle calls (`initialize`, `session/new`, `session/prompt`, `session/end`), and exposes ACP orchestration to RayClaw's primary LLM through local wrapper tools named `acp_coding`, `acp_new_session`, `acp_prompt`, `acp_end_session`, `acp_list_sessions`, `acp_submit_job`, and `acp_job_status`.
69	
70	Those `acp_*` names are RayClaw's local tool registry convention, not ACP. RayClaw's ACP tool-call handling treats incoming `session/update` tool calls as observations and records them by title/raw input. It does not dispatch those reported ACP tool calls through RayClaw's local registry.
71	
72	Important evidence:
73	
74	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs:436-453` initializes ACP with client capabilities, not a host tool registry.
75	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs:752-843` handles `session/request_permission`.
76	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs:846-984` parses ACP tool-call progress.
77	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs:1521-1531` creates sessions with `mcpServers: []`.
78	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/tools/acp.rs:16-40` registers the local `acp_*` wrapper tools.
79	- `.resources/rayclaw/tests/acp_integration.rs:128-240` enforces local tool-name uniqueness, allowed characters, length, and collision checks.
80	
81	Transferable points:
82	
83	- Separate ACP orchestration tools from normal runtime tools.
84	- Treat ACP tool calls as child-agent telemetry unless AGH deliberately bridges them.
85	- Do not prefer `allow_always` as an automatic approval default the way RayClaw does under `auto_approve`; AGH should keep durable grants explicit.
86	- Reject or disambiguate sanitized name collisions rather than truncating.
87	
88	### Harnss
89	
90	Harnss is an ACP client/orchestrator with an Electron bridge and React UI. It converts renderer MCP server configs into ACP SDK `McpServer[]`, including stdio and remote transports, then passes them into `newSession` and `loadSession`. It also supports live reload through ACP `loadSession` when available.
91	
92	Harnss does not consume or expose an ACP tool registry. Its "registry" evidence is an ACP agent registry and a UI-side static MCP renderer table. ACP tool calls are converted into UI messages keyed by `toolCallId`, using title/kind/raw input/output normalization.
93	
94	Important evidence:

112	### ACPX
113	
114	ACPX is a headless ACP client/orchestrator. Its registry is an agent/adapter registry mapping names like `codex`, `claude`, `gemini`, and others to launch commands. This is not a callable tool registry.
115	
116	ACPX implements client authority callbacks such as filesystem read/write, terminal create/output/wait/kill/release, and `session/request_permission`. It parses `mcpServers` from config and passes them through to `session/new` and `session/load`. It does not discover or normalize MCP tools into a registry.
117	
118	Important evidence:
119	
120	- `.resources/acpx/src/agent-registry.ts:38-107` maps adapter names to commands.
121	- `.resources/acpx/src/mcp-servers.ts:100-177` parses MCP server configs.
122	- `.resources/acpx/src/acp/client.ts:475-538` wires ACP client callbacks and initialize capabilities.
123	- `.resources/acpx/src/acp/client.ts:638-693` passes `mcpServers` to `session/new` and `session/load`.
124	- `.resources/acpx/src/permissions.ts:98-152` implements coarse permission decisions.

126	- `.resources/acpx/conformance/cases/021-prompt-post-success-drain.json:1-50` shows late tool updates can arrive after prompt success.

129	
130	- Keep agent/provider registries separate from Tool Registry.
131	- ACP compatibility includes filesystem and terminal callbacks where advertised; those callbacks must share AGH's registry policy engine or be routed through equivalent approval gates.
132	- Preserve distinct identities: AGH record IDs, ACP session IDs, provider-native session IDs, tool call IDs, and registry tool IDs.
133	- Add a protocol-aware drain/settle window for late `tool_call_update` events.

135	### OpenClaw
136	
137	OpenClaw's main `openclaw acp` bridge is a Gateway-backed ACP server. It forwards prompts to the Gateway and translates Gateway events into ACP session updates. The main bridge advertises MCP HTTP/SSE support as disabled and rejects non-empty per-session `mcpServers`; its docs say MCP should be configured at the Gateway/agent layer.
138	
139	OpenClaw has rich internal tool catalogs and plugin/MCP surfaces, but the main ACP bridge does not expose them as an ACP tool registry. Tool identity in ACP is display/event identity: title formatting plus inferred `ToolKind`.

158	### OpenCode
159	
160	OpenCode implements a native ACP server and has a real internal `ToolRegistry`. This is the strongest local reference for how an agent can maintain a rich internal registry while ACP still sees only session lifecycle, MCP bootstrap, tool-call updates, and permission requests.
161	
162	OpenCode accepts ACP per-session `mcpServers`, stores them in ACP session state, converts them into internal MCP config, and adds them through its SDK. Its internal MCP naming pattern exposes MCP tools as `sanitize(server) + "_" + sanitize(tool)` while preserving the original MCP tool name for the actual call.

180	- Preserve raw MCP server/tool names separately from the canonical AGH `ToolID`.
181	- Emit a stable ACP lifecycle, preferably `pending -> in_progress -> completed/failed`, even when the underlying runtime first reports a running event.
182	- Do not rely on a single-underscore sanitized naming scheme without collision diagnostics.

184	### Hermes, Multica, GoClaw, Sandbox-Agent, T3Code
185	
186	These projects reinforce the same split:
187	
188	- `.resources/hermes` is relevant because it registers ACP-provided MCP servers into agent state and valid tool names, then maps tool events into ACP kinds/titles. It has useful registry ideas, but ACP remains the session/event layer.
189	- `.resources/multica` and `.resources/goclaw` normalize ACP tool titles/kinds for display and permission heuristics; neither shows a protocol-level tool registry.
190	- `.resources/sandbox-agent` has ACP HTTP-to-stdio adapter and launch registry logic, but no callable ACP tool registry.
191	- `.resources/t3code` provides ACP schema/client/runtime tracking for tool-call events, not a broad registry.
192	
193	## Design Implications For AGH
194	
195	1. ACP compatibility is not a reason to avoid a daemon Tool Registry. ACP leaves tool discovery/execution models to the agent/runtime, or to MCP servers supplied to the session.
196	
197	2. AGH should expose daemon-owned session tools through an AGH-hosted MCP server in the MVP. This matches the accepted ADR-002 direction and aligns with ACP's `mcpServers` field.
198	
199	3. The registry's canonical ID must not be ACP `title`. Use one stable provider-safe `ToolID` across AGH and hosted MCP.
200	
201	4. The registry should store one canonical callable identity plus metadata:
202	   - `ToolID`: provider-safe lower snake segments separated by reserved `__`, for example `agh__skill_view`.
203	   - `DisplayTitle`: user-facing title only.
204	   - `SourceRef`: structured provenance, for example built-in, MCP server, extension ID, bundle ID, provider ID.
205	
206	5. Collision handling must be fail-closed:
207	   - Canonical `ToolID` collision: provider registration error or conflicted diagnostic.
208	   - Sanitized external-name collision: tool is not exposed to the session until disambiguated.
209	   - Display title collision: allowed, because titles are not policy identities.
210	
211	6. Operator and session projections should remain separate:
212	   - Operator surfaces show unavailable, unauthorized, and conflicted tools with reason codes.
213	   - Session/model surfaces expose only callable tools after availability, authorization, approval ceiling, and collision checks.
214	
215	7. ACP permission policy integration must remain ceiling-based:
216	   - `deny-all` denies by default.
217	   - `approve-reads` auto-approves only registry-classified read-only tools and ACP read/search callbacks AGH classifies as read-only.
218	   - `approve-all` skips approval prompts for otherwise allowed tools, but does not bypass registry deny rules, extension grants, session lineage, source trust, availability, hooks, or conflict checks.
219	
220	8. ACP filesystem and terminal callbacks, if AGH advertises them, must not bypass Tool Registry policy. Either route them through the registry as first-class built-in tools or share the same policy/approval engine with equivalent telemetry and hooks.
221	
222	9. ACP `ToolKind` should be explicit metadata on AGH descriptors. Heuristics from title/kind are fallback-only for external ACP events that AGH observes but does not own.
223	
224	10. AGH should persist observed ACP tool calls separately from registry definitions. Observations are keyed by `toolCallId` within a session and carry title/kind/status/raw input/output. Registry entries are keyed by canonical `ToolID`.
225	
226	11. AGH should support late tool-call updates after prompt completion by draining the ACP event stream for a bounded window or until protocol-specific completion conditions are met.
227	
228	12. AGH should decide explicitly whether to accept third-party ACP `mcpServers` from clients:
229	    - If accepted, store them as session-scoped tool sources with clear precedence and conflict policy.
230	    - If rejected, document the OpenClaw-style stance and require MCP sources to be configured through AGH's registry/config lifecycle.
231	    - For this TechSpec, the safer MVP path is AGH-managed hosted MCP first, with acceptance of client-supplied MCP servers as a compatibility extension only if collision and source-trust rules are implemented.
232	
233	## Accepted Naming And Collision Recommendation
234	
235	Adopt one canonical public `ToolID` format:
236	
237	- Canonical ID: provider-safe lower snake segments separated by reserved double underscore, for example `agh__skill_view`, `agh__tool_search`, `mcp__github__create_issue`, `ext__linear__search`.
238	- Hosted MCP `Tool.name`: same as the canonical `ToolID`.
239	- Display title: human-readable and non-unique, for example `View Skill`.
240	- Source/provenance: structured fields, not inferred solely from name prefixes.
241	- No shadowing: providers cannot replace an existing canonical ID unless they are the same source updating the same record.
242	- No silent truncation: if sanitization or length rules would collide, registration/session projection marks the tool conflicted and hides it from the session surface.
243	- Policy, dispatch, telemetry, hooks, CLI, HTTP, UDS, and hosted MCP all use the same `ToolID`.
244	
245	This keeps AGH's internal registry expressive while respecting MCP wire compatibility and ACP's event-oriented model.

1	# Internal Backend (Go)
2	
3	The Go runtime — `internal/*` packages composed by `internal/daemon`, plus the API transports under `internal/api/*`. ACP subprocess management, SQLite persistence, HTTP/SSE + UDS APIs, autonomy kernel, AGH Network. Entry binary lives in `cmd/agh`.
4	
5	Repo-wide rules (Critical Rules, Workflow, Build, Commits, Skill Dispatch, Memory & Skills RFC, CI/Release) live in the **root `CLAUDE.md`**. This file owns architecture, package boundaries, autonomy contracts, security invariants, and `internal/`-specific debugging/forensics.
6	
7	## Architecture
8	
9	### Principles
10	
11	- **Designed for incremental extension** — new capabilities arrive as new packages wired into `daemon/`, without modifying existing packages. Small interfaces + dependency injection. Every capability plan decides which extension points, hooks, capabilities, tools/resources, bundles, registries, bridge SDKs, and docs must be added, updated, or removed.
12	- **Pragmatic Flat with Discipline** — packages under `internal/`, API transports grouped under `api/`, no domain/infra split, no event bus.
13	- **`daemon/` is the sole composition root** — the only package that imports all others. Reconciliation logic running at boot belongs to composition root and is not "legacy support".
14	- **No package imports `daemon/`, `api/`, or `cli/`** — dependencies flow downward only.
15	- **Interfaces defined where consumed** (Go-style) — `session/` defines `AgentDriver`, `acp/` implements it.
16	- **Direct function calls through interfaces** — no event bus, no reflection-based routing, no NATS as inter-package coordination. NATS is permitted **only** inside `internal/network` as the embedded wire transport for the AGH Network protocol; daemon packages communicate via interfaces and the Notifier pattern, never by publishing to subjects.
17	- **Notifier pattern for fan-out** — typed interface for observability and SSE, not a generic bus.
18	- **No back-pointers between packages** — inject callbacks or interfaces.
19	- **Functional options for constructors** — `NewManager(opts ...Option)`.
20	- **Maps for <10 items** — no registry interfaces for small collections.
21	- **File-level organization** within packages — sub-packages only when complexity justifies it.
22	- **CI-enforceable boundaries** — `mage Boundaries` rules prevent import cycles. Update `magefile.go` Boundaries() in the same commit that introduces a new `internal/api/*` subpackage.
23	- **`internal/api/core` is the canonical handler home.** REST/UDS endpoints exist as shared `BaseHandlers` methods; HTTP and UDS only choose registration and authentication. No transport-duplicated parsing/validation.
24	- **Authoritative primitives are exclusive.** When a primitive owns a state transition (`task.Service.ClaimNextRun`, `Spawn`, `EnsureMigration`), no peer package may replicate it. Wake/observe/sweep are allowed; claim/own is not. The mechanical scheduler does not call `ClaimNextRun`.
25	- **Hooks are typed dispatch, not an event bus.** Dispatch at the call site that owns the state transition. Never tail event/log tables to fire hooks. Hooks may deny/narrow/annotate but cannot bypass safety primitives (claim tokens, leases, TTL, lineage, spawn caps, permission narrowing).
26	- **Agent-manageable by default.** User-visible runtime capabilities must expose stable machine-readable control surfaces for agents: CLI verbs with `-o json`/`-o jsonl` where relevant, HTTP/UDS parity when state crosses the daemon boundary, discoverable status/config output, and docs that describe the agent path. UI-only manageability is incomplete.
27	- **No partial-surface completions.** Any change touching a public surface closes the loop end-to-end in one pass: contract → HTTP handler → UDS handler → CLI client → CLI command → extension/config/docs surfaces → tests → docs.
28	
29	### Concurrency
30	
31	Generic Go concurrency patterns (goroutine ownership, channels vs mutexes, `select`/`ctx.Done()` discipline, no `time.Sleep` in orchestration) live in `agh-code-guidelines`. Architectural invariants below are load-bearing for design decisions:
32	
33	- **Goroutines spawned by `internal/session/manager_*.go` MUST be tracked by Manager-owned WaitGroup and joined in Manager shutdown.** Never put goroutine-owned channels in a struct field that another goroutine mutates — use a per-run handle.
34	- **Detached execution lifetime.** Any work that outlives an HTTP/UDS request — prompts, network channel sends, automation jobs — MUST detach via `context.WithoutCancel(ctx)`. Never tie execution lifetime to request lifetime. Expose explicit cancel endpoints (e.g., `POST /api/sessions/:id/prompt/cancel`).
35	- **`context.WithoutCancel` does NOT preserve deadlines.** Re-attach a deadline if needed.
36	- **Subprocess managed-stop** must respect `ctx.Done()` between Shutdown and Wait. Wrap `proc.Wait()` in `select { case <-proc.Done(): case <-ctx.Done(): }`.
37	- **Process-group supervision parity.** Unix uses process groups; Windows uses forced-exit fallback. Always cross-build with `GOOS=windows GOARCH=amd64 go build` before claiming subprocess work complete. Centralize signaling helpers in `internal/procutil`.
38	
39	### Runtime
40	
41	- Single-binary and local-first. Sidecars or external control planes require a written techspec.
42	- Keep execution paths deterministic and observable.
43	- **Daemon runs in background by default.** No daemon should require a foreground terminal.
44	- **`compozy exec` is headless.** `--format text` returns a single string; `--format json` returns a stream of valid JSON objects; the TUI is opt-in via `--tui`. `exec` does not persist artifacts to `.compozy/runs/` unless `--persist` is given.
45	- **Agent operations must not depend on the web UI.** If agents need to inspect, configure, start, stop, approve, claim, release, or repair a capability, the spec must provide a CLI/HTTP/UDS path with structured output and deterministic errors.
46	
47	### Observability
48	
49	- Every domain operation emits a canonical event with correlation keys (`workspace_id`, `session_id`, `parent_session_id`, `root_session_id`, `agent_name`, `task_id`, `run_id`, `claim_token_hash`, `lease_until`, `workflow_id`, `coordinator_session_id`, `scheduler_reason`, `hook_event`, `hook_name`, `spawn_depth`, `actor_kind`, `actor_id`, `release_reason`).
50	- Cover with a coverage matrix test that fails if any required lifecycle path doesn't emit its canonical event.
51	- Append-only event store (`runtime.db`) is the canonical operational ledger; session DBs are projections, not authority.
52	- Live broadcasters publish only after durable append; reconnect/replay uses `after_seq`.
53	
54	## Security Invariants
55	
56	- **`claim_token` redaction is non-negotiable.** Raw `claim_token` (`agh_claim_*`), MCP auth tokens, OAuth codes, PKCE verifiers, and secret bindings MUST NEVER appear in logs, status APIs, settings views, error payloads, channel messages, SSE, web UI, or memory. Use hash forms (`claim_token_hash`) over the wire. Network layer rejects raw `claim_token` in metadata.
57	- **Symlink escape hardening.** Skill sidecars, skill files, managed-extension dependency copies, and bundle install paths MUST verify resolved targets remain inside approved roots. Use `EvalSymlinks` + path-prefix check, not naive joins. Handle macOS `/private/var/folders` quirk (canonicalize source root before containment check).
58	- **Path security helpers.** Filesystem helpers resolving user-controlled or agent-controlled paths use the `sanitizePathKey` + `realpathDeepestExisting` pattern (defenses against null-byte, URL-encoded traversal, Unicode normalization, symlink-escape).
59	- **Identity proof-stripping defense.** In any signed-message processing path (AGH Network v1), an identity in verified format (`nickname@fingerprint`) without valid `proof` MUST classify as `rejected`, not `unverified`.
60	- **External-call timeouts.** Outbound HTTP/network calls MUST use a client with an explicit timeout. `http.DefaultClient` is forbidden in production code paths.
61	- **Load-time security scan.** Every non-bundled skill is scanned via `internal/skills.VerifyContent` on every load (not just install). Critical findings block; warning findings log; info findings log silently. Bundled skills are exempt because `go:embed` provides immutability.

123	## Memory & Skills Runtime (RFC-backed)
124	
125	- **Five-layer skill/memory/agent precedence**: Bundled → Marketplace → User → Additional → Workspace, with agent-local overriding all. Higher precedence wins on collision; an audit trail logs every shadow.
126	- **Memory taxonomy**: `user | feedback | project | reference` types; scopes `agent | workspace | global`. Default write scope declared per agent in `memory.scope`.
127	- **Memory consolidation gates**: Time → Sessions → Lock cascade ordered by computational cost. Default gates: 24h, 5 touched sessions, file-lock. Never replace gates with naive heuristics.
128	- **Lifecycle hooks** (`on_session_created`, `on_session_stopped`) execute in hierarchy precedence then alphabetical order; configurable timeout (default 5s); fail-open semantics (errors logged, never block); JSON over stdin.
129	
130	## Forensic Bug Fixes
131	
132	- **Bug-fix plans open with confirmed reproduction** (timestamp, command, observed evidence) BEFORE listing changes. "I think" or "probably" is forbidden at the top of a fix plan.
133	- **Inactive metadata repair must distinguish startup-pending from crashed.** Sessions in `m.pending` are still starting, not failed.
134	- **Stale ACP session ids must be classified, not propagated.** Convert `Resource not found` to fresh-start fallback.
135

1	# Competitor Analysis: Claude Code Tool System
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Claude Code does not expose a single mutable "ToolRegistry" class in the generic plugin sense. Its effective registry is a layered system:
6	
7	1. a strongly typed `Tool` contract,
8	2. a static built-in tool list,
9	3. request-time tool pool assembly,
10	4. MCP adaptation into the same contract,
11	5. deferred discovery through Tool Search,
12	6. centralized dispatch and permission handling.
13	
14	For AGH, the main lesson is not Claude Code's static `tools.ts` shape. The useful pattern is the separation between canonical definition, contribution adapters, context-specific assembly, permission decision, dispatch, result mapping, and dynamic discovery.
15	
16	## Mechanisms / Patterns
17	
18	The `Tool` interface carries model identity, schemas, runtime behavior, permission hooks, safety metadata, rendering hooks, output mapping, and dynamic discovery flags. `buildTool` applies defaults for omitted fields.
19	
20	Built-in tools are imported statically and exposed through `getAllBaseTools()`. `getTools(permissionContext)` filters this base list by mode, deny rules, special/internal tool rules, REPL visibility, and per-tool availability. `assembleToolPool()` then merges built-ins with MCP tools, sorts for prompt-cache stability, and deduplicates by name with built-ins winning.
21	
22	MCP tools are adapted into the same `Tool` contract. Claude Code calls MCP `tools/list`, maps schemas and annotations, preserves server/tool provenance in `mcpInfo`, and refreshes on `tools/list_changed`.
23	
24	Deferred discovery is a key pattern. Deferred tools are indexed by name/search hints and hidden from the initial API request. The model can call `ToolSearchTool`, which returns `tool_reference` blocks that cause the full schemas to be included later. Claude Code also has fallback delta/message paths when provider-native dynamic discovery is unavailable.
25	
26	Permissions are evaluated in an ordered policy pipeline. Deny rules can hide tools from visibility and block dispatch. Runtime permission checks consider explicit allow/deny/ask rules, tool-specific checks, user interaction requirements, safety classification, headless behavior, mode bypasses, and hooks.
27	
28	Dispatch is centralized. `runToolUse` resolves the tool, validates schema input, runs optional validation, prepares observable input, executes pre-tool hooks, resolves permission, calls the handler, maps results, and handles result-size/storage behavior. `StreamingToolExecutor` uses `isConcurrencySafe` to parallelize safe tools while serializing unsafe ones.
29	
30	Plugins contribute tool-like behavior primarily through MCP servers, skills, commands, agents, hooks, and settings rather than native in-process `Tool` objects. This is a useful extension boundary for AGH because it keeps third-party tools behind a protocol/process adapter.
31	
32	## Relevant Code Paths
33	
34	- `.resources/claude-code/Tool.ts:362-520`: canonical `Tool` fields including schemas, `call`, availability, read-only/destructive/concurrency metadata, user-interaction requirements, deferred-loading flags, MCP metadata, validation, and permissions.
35	- `.resources/claude-code/Tool.ts:701-783`: `ToolDef`, defaults, and `buildTool`.
36	- `.resources/claude-code/tools.ts:161-193`: built-in tool source of truth.
37	- `.resources/claude-code/tools.ts:262-326`: runtime filtering by deny rules, special tools, REPL visibility, and `isEnabled`.
38	- `.resources/claude-code/tools.ts:345-389`: built-in + MCP tool pool assembly and deduplication.
39	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/api.ts:123-234`: conversion to API tool schemas and deferred-loading fields.
40	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/toolSearch.ts:155-197`: Tool Search modes.
41	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/toolSearch.ts:270-385`: provider/model capability checks for Tool Search.
42	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/toolSearch.ts:525-646`: discovered deferred tool extraction and delta computation.
43	- `.resources/claude-code/tools/ToolSearchTool/ToolSearchTool.ts:167-471`: search scoring and `tool_reference` outputs.
44	- `.resources/claude-code/services/api/claude.ts:1120-1339`: request-time Tool Search enablement and fallback injection.
45	- `.resources/claude-code/services/tools/toolExecution.ts:340-1297`: central dispatch.
46	- `.resources/claude-code/services/tools/streamingToolExecutor.ts:35-391`: concurrency-safe scheduling.
47	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/permissions/permissions.ts:236-362`, `1067-1312`: rule matching and ordered permission engine.
48	- `.resources/claude-code/services/mcp/client.ts:1738-2010`, `2160-2195`, `3020-3075`: MCP tools/list ingestion, adaptation, refresh, and call execution.
49	- `.resources/claude-code/services/mcp/useManageMCPConnections.ts:600-690`: `tools/list_changed` handling.
50	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/plugins/mcpPluginIntegration.ts:100-634`: plugin MCP server extraction, scoping, env resolution, and contribution adapter.
51	- `.resources/claude-code/types/plugin.ts:14-67`: plugin shape.
52	- `.resources/claude-code/skills/loadSkillsDir.ts:185-335`: skill frontmatter, `allowed-tools`, and user-invocable visibility.
53	- `.resources/claude-code/tools/AgentTool/runAgent.ts:440-690`: agent-specific permission scoping and merged tool pools.
54	
55	## Transferable Patterns
56	
57	AGH should define a first-class tool definition contract that attaches identity, schema, provenance, visibility, availability, permission requirements, dispatch handler, output policy, and observability metadata.
58	
59	AGH should treat tool pool assembly as separate from registration. Registration collects contributions; assembly produces a context-specific view for a workspace, user, agent, session, provider, mode, runtime health, and permission policy.
60	
61	AGH should model visibility explicitly instead of scattering "hidden" flags. Candidate tiers include internal-only, daemon-manageable, CLI/HTTP-visible, agent-visible, model-visible, deferred-discoverable, user-command-only, and extension-private.
62	
63	AGH should support provider-neutral search and deferred schema loading even if Anthropic-specific `tool_reference` blocks are not portable. A registry search index and `agh__tool_search` are useful independently.
64	
65	AGH should keep permissions as an ordered pipeline. Discovery-time filtering improves UX, but dispatch must revalidate deny/ask/allow rules, tool-specific requirements, session permissions, workspace permissions, and hooks.
66	
67	AGH should map MCP annotations into local metadata at adapter boundaries: read-only, destructive, open-world, title, schema, search hints, and provenance.
68	
69	AGH should carry `IsConcurrencySafe` or equivalent execution metadata and enforce it centrally.
70	
71	## Risks / Mismatches
72	
73	Claude Code makes LLM API calls directly and can use provider-specific dynamic tool features. AGH usually manages external ACP-compatible runtimes, so it cannot assume direct control of model API request payloads.
74	
75	Claude Code's built-in registry is a static import list. AGH needs an extensible daemon registry with contribution adapters, not a monolithic static list.
76	
77	`buildTool` defaults some permission behavior in a way that is acceptable for controlled built-ins but too permissive for third-party extension tools. AGH should default untrusted external tools to disabled, deny, or ask until policy grants them.
78	
79	Claude Code uses naming conventions such as MCP prefixes for some behavior. AGH should use structured provenance and namespaces instead.
80	
81	Claude Code's plugin tools primarily flow through MCP. This is a good MVP boundary, but AGH may also want a native extension Host API for richer lifecycle and local-resource management.
82	
83	## Open Questions
84	
85	1. Should AGH expose native registry tools to ACP runtimes as hosted MCP tools, direct ACP tools, or CLI/UDS-callable commands?
86	2. Should AGH persist deferred-tool discovery state per session, or keep search stateless and recompute on each call?
87	3. What should happen when an ACP runtime already has a tool with the same short name as an AGH-native tool?
88	4. Should extension/plugin tool contributions be MCP-only for MVP?
89	
90	## Evidence
91	
92	- `.resources/claude-code/Tool.ts:362-520`, `701-783`: canonical tool shape and defaults.
93	- `.resources/claude-code/tools.ts:161-389`: base registry, filtering, MCP merge, sorting, dedupe.
94	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/api.ts:123-234`: schema projection and deferred-loading fields.
95	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/toolSearch.ts:155-646`: dynamic discovery logic.
96	- `.resources/claude-code/tools/ToolSearchTool/ToolSearchTool.ts:167-471`: search scoring and references.
97	- `.resources/claude-code/services/tools/toolExecution.ts:340-1297`: central dispatch and result mapping.
98	- `.resources/claude-code/services/tools/streamingToolExecutor.ts:35-391`: concurrency-safe scheduling.
99	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/permissions/permissions.ts:236-362`, `1067-1312`: permission rule engine.
100	- `.resources/claude-code/services/mcp/client.ts:1738-2010`, `2160-2195`, `3020-3075`: MCP adapter.
101	- `.resources/claude-code/utils/plugins/mcpPluginIntegration.ts:100-634`: plugin-to-MCP contribution adapter.
102

1	# Competitor Analysis: Hermes Tool Registry
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Hermes has the clearest direct precedent for the "ToolRegistry" name. Its tool system centers on a single Python registry that collects tool definitions, schemas, handlers, availability checks, toolset membership, MCP adapters, plugin contributions, and dispatch metadata.
6	
7	The core pattern is powerful but too global for AGH to copy literally. The transferable part is the product shape: every tool contribution normalizes into one registry contract, the model-visible tool list is filtered by availability and toolset policy, and tool calls flow through one dispatch path.
8	
9	## Mechanisms / Patterns
10	
11	Hermes tools self-register at import time. A tool module imports the singleton registry and calls `registry.register(...)` with a name, toolset, JSON schema, handler, optional `check_fn`, env requirements, display metadata, async flag, and result budget.
12	
13	The registry provides:
14	
15	- `register`: add a tool definition and reject most non-MCP collisions.
16	- `get`: resolve a tool entry.
17	- `dispatch`: call the registered handler.
18	- `get_available_tools`: project the model-visible list after toolset and availability filters.
19	
20	Toolsets are recursive bundles. Named toolsets can compose other toolsets, and broad aliases such as `all` / `*` expand across registered tools. This is the most relevant pattern for AGH's agent-role tool policy.
21	
22	Availability is attached to the tool definition through `check_fn` and `requires_env`. Hermes filters unavailable tools before presenting definitions to the model. This is a critical reliability property, but Hermes does not consistently treat it as a dispatch-time security boundary.
23	
24	MCP tools are adapted into the same registry. Hermes discovers MCP tools, normalizes schemas, prefixes/organizes names, refreshes on MCP `tools/list_changed`, and registers each remote tool as a local registry entry. Dynamic MCP refresh is tested.
25	
26	Plugins can contribute tools, but the exact dispatch path can bypass parts of the normal `handle_function_call` pipeline. AGH should avoid this split by making the registry dispatch path mandatory for every executable AGH-owned tool.
27	
28	## Relevant Code Paths
29	
30	- `.resources/hermes/tools/registry.py:1-14`: registry purpose and top-level contract.
31	- `.resources/hermes/tools/registry.py:23-64`: `ToolEntry` shape.
32	- `.resources/hermes/tools/registry.py:176-228`: singleton registry operations.
33	- `.resources/hermes/tools/registry.py:260-327`: availability filtering and definitions.
34	- `.resources/hermes/tools/registry.py:352-433`: dispatch and result handling.
35	- `.resources/hermes/model_tools.py:141-153`: built-ins are imported to trigger registration.
36	- `.resources/hermes/model_tools.py:209-370`: tool definition projection for model calls.
37	- `.resources/hermes/model_tools.py:389-528`: function-call handling.
38	- `.resources/hermes/model_tools.py:529-705`: result transformation and tool-call lifecycle.
39	- `.resources/hermes/toolsets.py:483-692`: recursive toolset composition.
40	- `.resources/hermes/hermes_cli/tools_config.py:681-849`: operator configuration for tools.
41	- `.resources/hermes/hermes_cli/plugins.py:210-380`: plugin load and metadata paths.
42	- `.resources/hermes/hermes_cli/plugins.py:518-646`: plugin tool dispatch path.
43	- `.resources/hermes/tools/mcp_tool.py:860-1038`: MCP discovery and schema adaptation.
44	- `.resources/hermes/tools/mcp_tool.py:1058-1296`: MCP tool registration details.
45	- `.resources/hermes/tools/mcp_tool.py:1850-2108`: MCP refresh and runtime paths.
46	- `.resources/hermes/tools/mcp_tool.py:2508-2770`: dynamic discovery integration.
47	- `.resources/hermes/tests/tools/test_mcp_dynamic_discovery.py:1-160`: tests for MCP dynamic tool refresh.
48	- `.resources/hermes/tools/process_registry.py:1-21`, `465-690`: process registry and scoped runtime management.
49	
50	## Transferable Patterns
51	
52	AGH should build one registry contract that all tool sources normalize into: built-in Go tools, extension manifest tools, extension sidecar tools, MCP tools, and future dynamic tools.
53	
54	AGH should separate toolset policy from tool definitions. Toolsets should be named bundles resolved recursively at list/dispatch time, not hardcoded into each provider.
55	
56	AGH should fail closed during discovery when required env vars, binaries, MCP servers, or extension sidecars are missing. The discovery surface should explain why a tool is unavailable to operators, while the agent-visible surface should omit or mark tools according to policy.
57	
58	AGH should route MCP tools through the same registry dispatch path as native tools. MCP is an adapter, not a separate tool universe.
59	
60	AGH should treat dynamic refresh as a first-class event. Hermes' MCP `tools/list_changed` path is a useful precedent for a registry generation counter and catalog delta notification.
61	
62	AGH should include result budgets and transformation at the registry boundary. Large outputs should be persisted or summarized consistently rather than left to individual handlers.
63	
64	## Risks / Mismatches
65	
66	Hermes relies heavily on a process-wide singleton and import-time registration. AGH should prefer explicit composition-root registration because daemon boot already wires skills, extensions, resource stores, hooks, API services, and session managers.
67	
68	Hermes uses permissive in-process plugin execution. AGH should not load third-party executable handlers into the daemon process for MVP. Out-of-process MCP or extension sidecar execution is a better fit for AGH's security and observability model.
69	
70	Hermes availability filtering is strong for model-visible definitions, but AGH must also recheck availability at dispatch. Discovery filtering alone is not a security boundary.
71	
72	Hermes has some name-prefix and collision behavior around MCP tools. AGH should use structured namespaces and provenance instead of deriving security meaning from string prefixes.
73	
74	Hermes' plugin dispatch split is a warning. If AGH has CLI, HTTP, UDS, session, MCP, and extension entry points, all of them must call the same `Registry.Call` pipeline.
75	
76	## Open Questions
77	
78	1. Should AGH expose unavailable tools to operators with reasons while hiding them from model-visible surfaces?
79	2. Should AGH support recursive toolsets as resources, config fields, or both?
80	3. Should MCP `tools/list_changed` cause live session deltas, or only refresh the next model-visible catalog query?
81	4. What conflict policy should AGH use when multiple providers contribute the same short tool name?
82	
83	## Evidence
84	
85	- Hermes registry: `.resources/hermes/tools/registry.py:23-64`, `176-228`, `260-327`, `352-433`.
86	- Hermes request pipeline: `.resources/hermes/model_tools.py:141-705`.
87	- Hermes toolsets: `.resources/hermes/toolsets.py:483-692`.
88	- Hermes MCP adapter: `.resources/hermes/tools/mcp_tool.py:860-1296`, `1850-2108`, `2508-2770`.
89	- Hermes plugin loader/dispatch: `.resources/hermes/hermes_cli/plugins.py:210-380`, `518-646`.
90	- Hermes dynamic discovery tests: `.resources/hermes/tests/tools/test_mcp_dynamic_discovery.py:1-160`.
91	- Hermes process registry: `.resources/hermes/tools/process_registry.py:1-21`, `465-690`.
92

1	# L-012 — TechSpec without Go interface signatures triggers heavy review rework
2	
3	**Class:** Spec authoring
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 / 2026-04-26 (autonomy techspec vs. release-adjustments comparison)
5	**Evidence sources:** `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` §PRD/TechSpec Quality Patterns
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	Two TechSpecs from the same period delivered radically different review trajectories:
10	
11	- **Autonomy techspec** (`.compozy/tasks/autonomous/_techspec.md`, 706 lines): MVP boundary at top, listed Architectural Boundaries, Go interface signatures pasted as code blocks (`ClaimCriteria`, `ClaimedRun`, `TaskClaimer`, `SpawnOpts`, `PermissionNarrower`), data-model fields with rationale, side-table-vs-JSON decisions explicit, lease invariants enumerated as a numbered list. Eighteen tasks executed cleanly with **one** review round.
12	- **Release-adjustments / qa-review** (no `_techspec.md`, just review-only directories): unresolved review queues persisted across multiple PRs. Tasks 07-09 of autonomy that touched contract-laden interfaces had **exactly one** round of fixes because the techspec gave the implementer no contract ambiguity.
13	
14	The differentiator was not length — it was concreteness. Specs that paste signatures, list fields with rationale, and enumerate invariants leave nothing to interpretation. Specs that describe the same mechanics in prose force the implementer to invent shapes that reviewers then reject.
15	
16	## Root cause
17	
18	Prose-only descriptions produce N implementations, where N is the number of agents that read the spec. Reviewers then converge each implementation toward the implicit intent through review rounds — that is the rework. Code blocks (interface signatures, struct fields, SQL DDL, enum values) are unambiguous; reviewers either approve or reject specific tokens, and the spec author resolves the ambiguity once instead of N times.
19	
20	## Rule
21	
22	> A TechSpec is not ready for review until it carries the **six quality markers**:
23	>
24	> 1. MVP boundary statement at top.
25	> 2. Architectural Boundaries section.
26	> 3. Concrete Go interface signatures pasted as code blocks (not prose).
27	> 4. Data-model field rationale (purpose + shape per new column / frontmatter field / config key).
28	> 5. Side-table-vs-JSON decision stated for every new domain entity.
29	> 6. Lease / safety invariants as a numbered list.
30	>
31	> Specs without these markers are pre-rejected — they will need multiple review rounds.
32	
33	## Operationalization
34	
35	`cy-spec-peer-review` invokes Opus with a six-marker checklist embedded in the prompt. `cy-spec-preflight` blocks `cy-create-techspec` from completing until the six markers are present.
36	
37	When a spec is missing a marker, fix the spec — do not start tasks against the gap.
38	
39	## Anti-patterns
40	
41	- "The function will accept the relevant config and return the result." (no signature)
42	- "Add a column for ownership tracking." (no rationale, no name, no type)
43	- "Choose the appropriate storage shape." (no decision)
44	- "Ensure the lease is held safely." (no invariants)
45	- "We'll figure out the interface during implementation." (defers the ambiguity)
46	
47	## Source
48	
49	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/_techspec.md` — the cleanest exemplar
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-003.md` (lease invariants numbered)
51	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-006.md` (safe spawn caps numbered)
52	- `.compozy/tasks/release-adjustments/` and `.compozy/tasks/qa-review/` — review-only dirs without TechSpecs, persistent unresolved review queues
53	- `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` §"Markers of 'good enough to execute'" and §"Markers of trouble"
54	- `docs/_memory/_synthesis.md` skill candidate S-M9 `agh-techspec-quality-gate`
55

1	# L-005 — Authoritative primitives are exclusive
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (autonomy ADR-004); reinforced 2026-04-26 (autonomy task_11)
5	**Evidence sources:** ADR-004 + task_11 memory + global_runs analysis
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The mechanical scheduler in `internal/scheduler` was tempted to claim runs directly during sweeps and recoveries. That would have collapsed the agent-pull model into a daemon-push model and given two components authority over the same state transition.
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	When two components can perform the same authoritative state transition (claim, spawn, migrate, narrow), they will eventually disagree. Either you serialize them (introducing locks, latency, and complexity), or you accept two sources of truth (introducing races and recovery bugs). The clean answer is to pick one authority per transition and force everything else to _observe and notify_ rather than _act_.
14	
15	## Rule
16	
17	> When an authoritative primitive owns a state transition (`task.Service.ClaimNextRun`, `Spawn`, `EnsureMigration`), no peer package may replicate the transition. Wake/observe/sweep are allowed; claim/own is not.
18	
19	## Examples (canonical authorities in AGH)
20	
21	| Transition                    | Authority                            | Allowed peers                                                  |
22	| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
23	| Claim a `task_run`            | `task.Service.ClaimNextRun`          | `internal/scheduler` may wake idle agents; never claims itself |
24	| Spawn a child session         | Daemon-managed safe-spawn API        | Coordinator submits requests; the daemon decides               |
25	| Apply a schema migration      | `internal/store` migrations registry | `EnsureSchema` is forbidden for column changes                 |
26	| Mutate session terminal state | Session manager                      | Channels, hooks, observability emit events but cannot mutate   |
27	| Approve / publish a task      | Operator + manual API surface        | Coordinators receive enqueues; do not auto-approve             |
28	
29	## Operationalization
30	
31	- **Scheduler can wake and sweep, but cannot claim.** `internal/scheduler` issues `scheduler.wake.count`/`no_match`/`lease_sweep.count`/`error` metrics — never `task.run.claim.success`.
32	- **Hooks can deny/narrow/annotate but cannot bypass safety invariants** (claim tokens, leases, TTL, lineage, spawn caps, permission narrowing).
33	- **Coordination channels are NEVER an ownership/status authority.** Channel `status`/`result` messages cannot mutate ownership/terminal state.
34	
35	## Anti-pattern
36	
37	- Adding a "scheduler.\*" hook taxonomy that lets external code claim runs.
38	- Letting the coordinator bypass `ClaimNextRun` through a "fast-path" for trusted runs.
39	- Allowing the network layer to write terminal state via channel messages.
40	
41	## Source
42	
43	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-004.md` — Split Semantic vs Mechanical Scheduling
44	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-009.md` — Hooks First-Class (forbids hook-as-claim)
45	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_11.md` — scheduler scope-creep avoided
46	- `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` lesson L4
47

1	# L-006 — Greenfield + zero-legacy means _delete_, not _adapt_
2	
3	**Class:** Project posture
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-17 (harness TechSpec review, Portuguese-language reviewer)
5	**Evidence sources:** Harness review + `remove-legacy-alpha.md` standing directive + multiple ADRs
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The harness TechSpec proposed migrating an `inputAugmenter` callback to a `TurnAugmenter` pipeline. The spec did not say whether the old callback was deleted, kept as an adapter, or coexisting. The reviewer (in Portuguese) flagged this directly: _"política zero-legacy exige declarar 'delete'"_ — the zero-legacy policy _requires_ the spec to declare what is deleted.
10	
11	This is a stronger application of the CLAUDE.md "Greenfield Alpha — Zero Legacy Tolerance" rule: it's not enough to _allow_ deletion; specs must _enumerate_ what is deleted.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	When a spec says "we are migrating to X" without naming the delete-target, agents default to keeping both. Compatibility shims, adapters, and "preserve old behavior" branches accumulate as technical debt. Greenfield discipline only works if every breaking-change spec explicitly names what disappears.
16	
17	## Rule
18	
19	> Every breaking-change techspec MUST explicitly name its delete targets. "Delete the old thing" is not a default; it is a checklist item that must be enumerated.
20	
21	## Operationalization
22	
23	In every TechSpec that changes a public surface (or any meaningful internal contract), include a section like:
24	
25	```markdown
26	## Delete Targets
27	
28	- `internal/foo.OldType` (replaced by `internal/foo.NewType` in step 3)
29	- `pkg/bar.LegacyAdapter` (no callers after migration; remove in step 5)
30	- TOML key `[old.section]` (renamed; no backward alias)
31	- HTTP endpoint `/v0/old/path` (replaced by `/v1/new/path`; no redirect)
32	```
33	
34	Renames sweep code, storage, APIs, CLI, extensions, specs, RFCs, AND `.compozy/tasks/*` artifacts in the same change. No aliases, no dual fields, no migration code.
35	
36	## Allowed exception (single-pass repair)
37	
38	When the cost of "delete the old thing" is "every developer rebuilds their local SQLite," in-place ALTER + one-shot repair is allowed if and only if:
39	
40	1. Repair is bounded to a single boot.
41	2. Strict semantics resume immediately after repair.
42	3. The exception is documented in an ADR.
43	
44	Reference: `session-driver-override/adrs/adr-005.md`.
45	
46	## Source

1	# L-008 — Schema migrations are required even on fresh DBs
2	
3	**Class:** Persistence
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (Hermes BUG-002, Critical)
5	**Evidence sources:** Hermes BUG-002 + multiple Hermes/autonomy review issues
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The Hermes track widened the `memory_operation_log` table to add `scope`, `workspace_root`, `filename` columns. The change worked on fresh installs because `internal/memory/catalog.go` boots through `storepkg.EnsureSchema`, which created the new shape. But existing databases kept the old five-column table — `EnsureSchema` skipped existing tables — and `agh memory write` failed on upgrade with `no such column: scope`.
10	
11	CodeRabbit flagged it as Critical. The fix: schema migration v6 added through the deterministic migrations runner.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	`EnsureSchema`-style boot reconciliation has a fundamental gap: it creates tables that don't exist but does not mutate tables that do. Any column/index/constraint addition needs a real migration; a migration is required _even when fresh installs already work_, because upgrade is a first-class scenario in alpha.
16	
17	A second contributor: AGH had two schema paths — the global DB used a real migrations registry, while the catalog DB used `EnsureSchema`. Drift between these paths produced the bug.
18	
19	## Rule
20	
21	> Any change to a SQLite column, index, or constraint MUST add a versioned migration in the migrations registry. `EnsureSchema`-style boot reconciliation is forbidden for column changes. Test fresh-DB AND reopen-after-restart paths.
22	
23	## Operationalization
24	
25	- **One schema migration primitive shared by all SQLite databases** (`agh.db`, `events.db`, catalog DBs, automation scheduler state, memory operation log).
26	- **Numbered migrations** are recorded in `schema_migrations`. Idempotent. Wrapped in transactions.
27	- **Test the upgrade path:** every schema change must include a `Test*FreshDB` test AND a `Test*ReopenAfterRestart` test.
28	- **`SQLite recovery code paths must rename or remove `-wal`and`-shm`companions, not only the`.db` file.** (Cross-cutting hygiene: see refac-v2 issue 001 Critical WAL recovery bug.)
29	- **Schema-version bump** lives in the migrations registry — do not encode it in code constants.
30	
31	## Allowed exception
32	
33	In greenfield alpha, a hard-cut rename + table rewrite without compat migration is allowed when:
34	
35	1. The change is documented in the techspec's "Delete Targets" section.
36	2. All callers of the old shape are deleted in the same change.
37	3. Per-developer wipe of local SQLite is acceptable cost.
38	
39	## Anti-pattern
40	
41	- `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS new_columns ...` then expecting the table to grow.
42	- Schema-version constants in Go code that don't match the migrations registry.
43	- Tests that only cover fresh-DB.
44	
45	## Source
46	
47	- `.codex/ledger/2026-04-25-MEMORY-hermes-qa-execution.md` (BUG-002)
48	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/reviews-001/issue_020.md` (Critical)
49	- `.compozy/tasks/refac-v2/reviews-001/issue_001.md` (WAL/SHM Critical)
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_07.md` (claim/lease schema v7)
51	- `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` lesson L1, `../analysis/analysis_local_runs.md` lesson LL-2
52

1	# L-007 — E2E harness regressions follow runtime contract changes
2	
3	**Class:** Testing
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-26 (autonomy task_18 QA)
5	**Evidence sources:** task_18 BUG-001/002/003 + global_runs
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The autonomy MVP `make verify` passed. Real-scenario QA via `real-scenario-qa` then surfaced three Playwright/E2E regressions:
10	
11	- **BUG-001** — workspace onboarding race in the web E2E `TC-AUTO-015` test; absent shared workspace-onboarding wait helper.
12	- **BUG-002** — `acpmock` exact-match canonicalization for situation-augmented prompts. Task 04 added a situation-context augmenter that changed the rendered prompt; the deterministic ACP mock fixture matcher still expected the pre-Task-04 shape.
13	- **BUG-003** — Tasks browser E2E asserting an empty Agents-panel state; manual-first publish actually rendered an active run.
14	
15	All three were rooted in tests written against an _older_ runtime contract. None was a production bug.
16	
17	## Root cause
18	
19	When a runtime contract changes — a new prompt augmenter, a different fixture canonicalization, a new manual-first UI state — the deterministic test infrastructure (acpmock fixtures, Playwright selectors, browser fixtures) embeds the _old_ contract. `make verify` passes because tests still hit their old expectations. Real-scenario QA exposes the drift.
20	
21	## Rule
22	
23	> When a runtime contract changes (prompt augmenter, situation context, fixture format, manual-first UI state), the E2E mock and matchers ship in the same PR. Do not let the test infrastructure encode a stale contract.
24	
25	## Operationalization
26	
27	- For ACP fixture work: replace fragile string-matching with structured prompt metadata. acpmock uses typed metadata, not rendered prompt substrings.
28	- For Playwright E2E: add shared wait helpers (`web/e2e/fixtures/selectors.ts`) for workspace onboarding, session creation, manual-first publish. New runtime states require helper updates in the same PR.
29	- Real-scenario QA is the canonical regression net. `make verify` is necessary but not sufficient.
30	- E2E regressions surfaced in the QA pass are NOT production bugs unless they reveal divergent runtime behavior. Fix the test infrastructure, not the runtime.
31	
32	## Anti-pattern
33	
34	- Adding a `time.Sleep(2 * time.Second)` to "stabilize" a flaky Playwright spec.
35	- Loosening an acpmock matcher to substring instead of metadata.
36	- Skipping E2E in the QA pass because "the unit tests cover it."
37	
38	## Source
39	
40	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_18.md`
41	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/qa/verification-report.md`
42	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/qa/issues/BUG-001.md`, `BUG-002.md`, `BUG-003.md`

1	# L-013 — PRD must not name frameworks, storage, error codes, or file formats
2	
3	**Class:** Spec authoring
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-18 (todo-api smux pairing run)
5	**Evidence sources:** Codex orchestrator prompt template + analysis_codex_sessions
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	In the `todo-api` smux pairing experiment, Pedro built an orchestrator role for one Claude pane that explicitly inspects PRDs and rejects any document that surfaces implementation choices. The orchestrator instruction was: _"PRD naming frameworks/storage engines/file formats — strip, push to TechSpec."_ Pedro adopted this as a generally-applied rule across AGH spec authoring.
10	
11	A PRD that names `PostgreSQL`, `react-query`, `OAuth 2.0`, `JWT`, `gRPC`, or specific HTTP error codes leaks implementation into the vision document. The implementer reads the PRD with framework constraints already locked in and stops asking "is this the right shape?" The TechSpec phase exists exactly to make those decisions — moving them earlier collapses two phases into one and removes the option to choose differently when the architecture surface comes into focus.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	LLM-authored PRDs default to "concrete and useful" framing because that's how product writing reads online. Real product writing is meant to ship a feature; AGH PRDs feed into a TechSpec that an architecture-aware reviewer will pressure-test. The PRD's job is to constrain the _user-observable_ surface, not the _implementation_ surface.
16	
17	## Rule
18	
19	> PRDs frame **what** and **why**, never **how**. PRDs MUST NOT name:
20	>
21	> - Frameworks (`react`, `next.js`, `tanstack-query`, `gin`, `cobra`, `gorm`).
22	> - Storage engines (`PostgreSQL`, `SQLite`, `Redis`, `S3`).
23	> - Wire protocols (`gRPC`, `JSON-RPC`, `WebSocket`).
24	> - Auth standards (`OAuth 2.0`, `JWT`, `mTLS`, `PKCE`).
25	> - File formats (`YAML`, `JSON`, `TOML`, `Protobuf`).
26	> - HTTP error codes / status numbers.
27	> - SQL schema or column names.
28	> - Specific tools (`bun`, `mise`, `goreleaser`).
29	>
30	> Strip and push to the TechSpec. Approval owner: PRD author.
31	
32	## Operationalization
33	
34	`cy-spec-preflight` runs a regex pass over the PRD draft and surfaces any matching tokens. Items found are listed for the author to either justify (rare exception, e.g., when the PRD is _about_ AGH Network's wire format) or strip.
35	
36	PRDs may name **product surfaces** (CLI verb, web route, doc page) when those are user-observable. They may not name the implementation behind those surfaces.
37	
38	## Anti-patterns
39	
40	- "Use PostgreSQL for the durable queue." → strip; TechSpec decides.
41	- "Return 422 when validation fails." → strip; TechSpec decides.
42	- "JWT-based session tokens." → strip; TechSpec decides.
43	- "React-query mutation for the publish action." → strip; TechSpec decides.
44	- "Store config in a YAML file." → strip; TechSpec decides whether YAML, TOML, or sidecar JSON.
45	
46	## Allowed exceptions
47	
48	- AGH Network protocol PRDs that are _about_ wire format (capability envelopes, NATS subjects). The protocol IS the user-observable surface for that PRD.
49	- AGENT.md / MEMORY.md / SKILL.md PRDs where the file format is the product.
50	- PRDs scoped to a specific framework's ergonomics (e.g., a PRD about TanStack Query usage patterns inside `web/`).
51	
52	## Source
53	
54	- `~/.codex/sessions/2026/04/18/19-27-52` (smux pairing orchestrator instruction)
55	- `../analysis/analysis_codex_sessions.md` §Anti-Patterns (todo-api smux rule)
56	- `docs/_memory/_synthesis.md` Top-level Finding 3
57

1	# L-003 — `task_runs` is the single durable work queue
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture / Autonomy
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (autonomy techspec, ADR-003)
5	**Evidence sources:** 3 ADRs forbid duplication
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	Designing the autonomy MVP, several alternatives proposed a parallel scheduler-owned queue ("scheduler claims, then asks task service to assign"). All were rejected.
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	Two durable queues for the same work creates two sources of truth for ownership state. Any divergence (lease vs. claim, sweep vs. heartbeat, recovery vs. boot) becomes a race. The existing `task_runs` table already carries `status`, `attempt`, `idempotency_key`, `origin_kind/ref` — adding state via columns and side tables is strictly cheaper than adding a parallel table.
14	
15	## Rule
16	
17	> `task_runs` is the single durable work queue. Do not introduce a parallel queue or actor table. Add new ownership/state via columns + side tables on `task_runs`.
18	
19	## Lease invariants (operationalization)
20	
21	1. Exactly one active claim token per non-terminal run.
22	2. Heartbeat/complete/fail/release compare run owner + claim token.
23	3. Stale/late after recovery fails explicitly (no silent reassignment).
24	4. Sweep + heartbeat serialize via SQLite tx (`BEGIN IMMEDIATE`).
25	5. Boot recovery runs BEFORE the scheduler accepts wake/claim traffic.
26	6. Lease extension is bounded by config.
27	7. One active lease per session in MVP.
28	
29	## Side-table strategy
30	
31	- Capability matching = exact-match rows in `task_run_required_capabilities` and `task_run_preferred_capabilities`.
32	- Coordination channels: `coordination_channel_id` column on `task_runs`.
33	- Permission narrowing data: side table indexed by `task_run_id`.
34	- **Never** stuff dynamic ownership/match state into a JSON metadata blob.
35	
36	## Source
37	
38	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-003.md` — Extend Task Runs for Atomic Claim/Lease
39	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-004.md` — Split Semantic vs Mechanical Scheduling
40	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-010.md` — Manual Operator Control First-Class

1	# L-004 — Manual operator paths converge with autonomous on the same primitives
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture / Autonomy
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (autonomy techspec, ADR-010 — most-repeated rule)
5	**Evidence sources:** ADR-010 referenced 7+ times across `_techspec.md`
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	Early autonomy drafts treated user-driven flows and agent-spawned flows as separate code paths. Pedro pushed back: "autonomy is additive, never replacement."
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	Splitting manual and autonomous into "user mode" and "agent mode" creates two implementations of every safety primitive (claim, lease, heartbeat, complete, fail, release, narrow). Inevitably they drift. Operators end up with weaker invariants than agents (or vice versa), and the system loses the property that operator and agent flows can interleave safely.
14	
15	## Rule
16	
17	> Manual operator paths and autonomous paths converge on the same primitives. User-created tasks, automation-created tasks, coordinator-created tasks, and agent-spawned child tasks all use the same task/run model and the same claim-token/lease/heartbeat/complete/fail/release rules.
18	
19	## Operationalization
20	
21	- **Task creation alone NEVER enqueues claimable work or starts the coordinator.** Publish/start/approval is the run-enqueue boundary that triggers coordinator bootstrap.
22	- **Operator commands are identity-explicit; agent commands are identity-implicit.** Operator endpoints MUST NOT infer agent identity from environment variables.
23	- **No separate manual/autonomous/coordinator queues.** All converge on `task_runs` with `actor_kind` differentiating origin.
24	- **E2E coverage MUST include both manual-first bookends:**
25	  1. `user create → publish → coordinated execution`
26	  2. `user-start session → direct prompt without coordinator`
27	- **Operator UI must visually distinguish creation vs. publish/approval vs. run enqueue vs. coordinator spawn.**
28	
29	## Source
30	
31	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-010.md` — most load-bearing autonomy ADR
32	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-005.md` — coordinator triggers (publish/start/approval)

1	# Local Ideas Cross-Reference: docs/ideas/from-claude-code
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	The `docs/ideas/from-claude-code` folder contains prior Claude Code analyses and a filtered recommendation document. The most important point is a tension:
6	
7	- Earlier filtering said Claude Code Tool Search and streaming tool execution were "not relevant" because AGH manages external ACP runtimes and does not make LLM API calls directly.
8	- A Tool Registry feature changes part of that conclusion. If AGH introduces daemon-owned, agent-callable tools, then search, progressive schema disclosure, permission ordering, result budgets, and tool metadata become relevant again.
9	
10	The TechSpec should be explicit about this boundary. AGH should not pretend it controls every provider's internal tool loop, but it can own a registry for AGH-native tools and extension-provided tools exposed through AGH surfaces.
11	
12	## Relevant Ideas
13	
14	### Canonical tool contract
15	
16	`analysis_tool_system.md` proposes a Go `Tool` shape with name, description, schema, permission checks, execution, classifier input, and result limits. This aligns with the competitor research, but AGH should avoid making security classifier behavior mandatory in the first registry layer.
17	
18	The stronger AGH version should split:
19	
20	- descriptor fields used for discovery and schema projection,
21	- policy metadata used for authorization,
22	- availability checks used for live health,
23	- handler/adapters used for dispatch,
24	- result policy used for truncation, persistence, redaction, and telemetry.
25	
26	### Tool Search and deferred loading
27	
28	The document proposes an `eager`, `deferred`, and `discovered` registry with `Search(query)` returning tool references. That is useful if AGH has large tool universes from MCP, extensions, skills, network peers, and built-ins.
29	
30	AGH should adapt this provider-neutrally:
31	
32	- `agh__tool_search` returns descriptors and optional schema handles.
33	- CLI/HTTP/UDS search returns the same data.
34	- ACP/model-specific `tool_reference` integration is optional and provider-dependent.
35	- The registry can still compute deltas and persist "discovered in this session" state later.
36	
37	### Result persistence and budgets
38	
39	The Claude Code analysis highlights per-tool max result sizes and disk persistence for large outputs. AGH needs the same concept because tool results may be delivered into session transcripts, HTTP responses, UDS clients, or agent-visible messages.
40	
41	The registry should define:
42	
43	- `MaxResultBytes` or a default by risk/source class,
44	- preview strategy,
45	- artifact persistence target,
46	- redaction path,
47	- telemetry fields for result bytes and persisted artifact id.
48	
49	### Permission and security validators
50	
51	The local idea files discuss command-specific validators, classifier input projection, dangerous pattern registries, and bash-specific semantics. Those are important for shell tools, but they should not block the Tool Registry foundation.
52	
53	For MVP, the registry should provide a hook point:
54	
55	- a tool can declare `RiskClass`, `OpenWorld`, `Destructive`, and `RequiresUserInteraction`;
56	- a policy engine can decide allow/deny/ask;
57	- specialized tools such as shell/url/browser can later plug in validators.
58	
59	### Prompt and catalog deltas
60	
61	`analysis_prompt_architecture.md` discusses enabled-tools-aware prompt sections and delta attachment patterns. The relevant AGH takeaway is that catalog changes should be incremental and explicit. For ACP runtimes that cannot accept live tool deltas, AGH should clearly fall back to "visible on next session."
62	
63	### Streaming executor and concurrency
64	
65	`analysis_query_engine.md` includes concurrency-safe vs exclusive tool execution. AGH should keep the metadata and enforce it at registry dispatch, but not copy a direct model streaming executor unless AGH owns a provider's query loop.
66	
67	### Plugin system references
68	
69	`analysis_services_infra.md` describes plugin refresh, availability, hooks, and plugin error taxonomy. This supports an AGH registry model where extension tools have lifecycle state and refresh reasons. AGH should convert extension sidecar and MCP health into availability reasons rather than exposing raw plugin errors to agents.
70	
71	## Filtered Recommendation Reversal
72	
73	`filtered_recommendations.md` says Tool Search, streaming execution, bash classifiers, and API tool loops are not relevant because AGH does not make LLM API calls. That remains true for driver-internal tools.
74	
75	However, the Tool Registry feature is not about controlling Claude Code's own tools. It is about creating AGH-owned tools that are:
76	
77	- discoverable through AGH,
78	- governed by AGH,
79	- executable through AGH,
80	- extensible by AGH extensions,
81	- visible to agents regardless of ACP runtime when an adapter exists.
82	
83	Therefore:
84	
85	- Do not copy Claude Code's provider API request mechanics into the MVP.
86	- Do copy the registry/search/permission/result architecture where AGH owns the tool surface.
87	
88	## Relevant Code / Document Paths
89	
90	- `docs/ideas/from-claude-code/analysis_tool_system.md:450-620`: deferred registry, security validator pipeline, classifier input projection, dangerous pattern registry, and key file references.
91	- `docs/ideas/from-claude-code/filtered_recommendations.md:1-38`: architectural warning that AGH is an orchestration kernel, not a direct LLM API loop.
92	- `docs/ideas/from-claude-code/analysis_prompt_architecture.md`: prompt sections and tool-aware catalog deltas.
93	- `docs/ideas/from-claude-code/analysis_query_engine.md`: tool execution concurrency and exclusive scheduling references.
94	- `docs/ideas/from-claude-code/analysis_services_infra.md`: plugin refresh and availability ideas.
95	
96	## Transferable Patterns
97	
98	1. Build a provider-neutral registry search API first.
99	2. Add deferred schema loading as an AGH catalog behavior, not as an Anthropic-only assumption.
100	3. Track per-tool result budgets and persisted artifacts.
101	4. Keep security validators pluggable by tool family.
102	5. Model concurrency metadata even before advanced scheduling.
103	6. Treat live catalog deltas as optional driver capabilities with a fallback.
104	
105	## Risks / Mismatches
106	
107	AGH should not duplicate the ACP runtime's internal shell/browser/file tools unless there is a clear cross-runtime AGH-owned reason.
108	
109	AGH should not depend on Claude Code-only `tool_reference` wire formats for the core registry contract.
110	
111	AGH should not make shell command classifiers part of the foundation unless the MVP includes an AGH-owned shell tool.
112	
113	AGH should not over-inject tool catalogs into prompts. Progressive disclosure and search should be preferred once the tool universe grows.
114	
115	## Open Questions
116	
117	1. Should AGH's first registry search surface be `agh__tool_search` as an agent-callable tool, `agh tool search` as CLI, or both?
118	2. Should catalog delta support be designed now even if only a subset of drivers implement it?
119	3. Should result persistence share AGH's session event/artifact store or get a dedicated tool-result artifact store?
120	
121	## Evidence

123	- `docs/ideas/from-claude-code/analysis_tool_system.md:450-620`: local implementation sketches for deferred registry, validators, result storage, and key Claude Code paths.

1	# Competitor Analysis: OpenClaw Tool and Plugin Registry
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	OpenClaw's strongest contribution is its two-phase extension model: manifest-first static discovery followed by runtime materialization. Plugin manifests can declare tool contracts, configuration, activation hints, and ownership metadata without immediately executing plugin code. Runtime tool registration then materializes concrete tools through plugin APIs or MCP adapters.
6	
7	This is highly relevant to AGH because AGH already lets extension manifests publish static `resources.tools`, but lacks a runtime execution boundary and a registry that can connect static declarations to callable backends.
8	
9	## Mechanisms / Patterns
10	
11	OpenClaw reads plugin manifests first. Static `contracts.tools`, config schema, activation hints, ownership, and duplicate diagnostics are available before runtime code is loaded. This supports cheap discovery, trust decisions, and policy projection.
12	
13	Runtime tools are registered through `api.registerTool` as either concrete tools or factories receiving a plugin tool context. This avoids global singleton state and gives each tool access to scoped runtime services.
14	
15	Tool materialization is policy-filtered. Core and plugin tools are assembled, then filtered by profile/provider/global/agent/group/sandbox/subagent rules. Plugin owner metadata supports policy by plugin id or broad plugin groups.
16	
17	OpenClaw treats MCP as a provider backend. Bundle/user MCP configs are connected over stdio, SSE, or streamable HTTP, tools are listed and sanitized, calls are wrapped, and per-session runtime instances have idle TTL and fingerprint invalidation.
18	
19	OpenClaw also exposes a direct HTTP gateway for tool invocation. This is useful as a manageability pattern, but AGH should only expose direct invoke through strict local authorization and the same registry dispatch pipeline.
20	
21	## Relevant Code Paths
22	
23	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/manifest.ts:250-367`: manifest contract shape.
24	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/manifest.ts:539-583`: manifest validation and duplicate handling.
25	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/manifest.ts:1161-1251`: manifest registry integration.
26	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/manifest-registry.ts:303-379`: plugin manifest discovery.
27	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/manifest-registry.ts:640-805`: precedence/diagnostics behavior.
28	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/types.ts:2209-2353`: plugin API and tool registration types.
29	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/tool-types.ts:8-45`: tool type definitions.
30	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/registry.ts:421-446`: plugin registry access patterns.
31	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/registry.ts:1464-1557`: activation/materialization path.
32	- `.resources/openclaw/src/plugins/tools.ts:111-239`: tool assembly from plugins.
33	- `.resources/openclaw/src/agents/pi-tools.ts:585-656`, `673-727`: policy-filtered agent tool projection.
